target.title: Add user chat bubble color selector for macOS application
target.body: # Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: The macOS chat bubble color is hardcoded to a static blue, with no way for users to personalize it. - Why it matters: Users want visual customization of the chat interface to match their preferences or distinguish between instances. - What changed: Added a new "Colors" settings tab with a 10-color palette picker, a custom `ColorPicker`, and a system accent color toggle. `ColorPreferencesStore` (in `OpenClawKit`) persists preferences via `UserDefaults` and exposes a `resolvedColor` that `ChatTheme.userBubble` now reads on macOS. `userText` dynamically picks black or white based on WCAG relative luminance. A cross-platform `ColorHexSupport` utility was added to `OpenClawKit` for hex-to-Color and Color-to-hex conversions. The macOS-local `ColorHexSupport` gained a reverse `hex(from:)` helper. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): iOS chat bubble color remains the existing static blue. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - macOS: New "Colors" tab in Settings (between "Channels" and "Skills") with three selection modes: system accent color (default, preserves existing behavior), palette color (10 curated colors from Apple's Human Interface Guidelines), or arbitrary custom color via `ColorPicker`. - Chat bubble color updates live when a new color is selected; preferences persist across app restarts via `UserDefaults`. - User message text automatically switches between white and black depending on the chosen bubble color's luminance (WCAG threshold at 0.6). ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user message] -> [hardcoded rgb(127,184,212) bubble] -> [white text] After: [user message] -> [ColorPreferencesStore.resolvedColor] -> [palette / custom / accent bubble] -> [WCAG luminance > 0.6?] -> yes -> [black text] -> no -> [white text] ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS 15+ - Runtime/container: Swift 6 / SwiftUI - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): N/A ### Steps 1. Build and launch the macOS app. 2. Open Settings > Colors. 3. Select a palette color, apply a custom color, or toggle "Use Accent Color." 4. Open a chat session see the selected color on your messages. ### Expected - User chat bubble reflects the selected color. - Text is white on dark bubbles and black on light bubbles (e.g. yellow). - Preference survives app restart. ### Actual - Verified as expected. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) **Color palette** **Custom color** **Dark text on bright color** ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Palette selection, custom color via ColorPicker, accent color toggle, preference persistence across restart, live bubble color update in chat. - Edge cases checked: Light colors (yellow, orange) produce black text; dark colors (indigo, blue) produce white text. Clearing custom color falls back to palette or accent. Off-main-thread `resolvedColor` defaults to light palette variant. - What you did **not** verify: iOS runtime (feature is macOS-only for now). No automated Swift tests were added for the new views or store. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: `ColorPreferencesStore.shared` is `nonisolated(unsafe)` and initialized eagerly in `AppState.init`; concurrent first access from a background thread could race. - Mitigation: `AppState` calls `_ = ColorPreferencesStore.shared` during init on the main actor, ensuring the singleton is created before any background reader. - Risk: `UserDefaults`-backed preferences do not sync across macOS reinstalls or profiles. - Mitigation: Defaults to system accent color (existing behavior); preferences are non-critical and easily re-set.

target.title: fix: close readline interface and destroy read stream in transcript store
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes a file descriptor leak in `TranscriptsStore.readUtterancesFromDir`. The `createReadStream` and `readline.createInterface` were not cleaned up after use. If `JSON.parse` threw inside the async iteration loop, the underlying file descriptor would remain open indefinitely.

## Why This Change Was Made

Wrap the `for await` loop in a `try/finally` block that explicitly calls `lines.close()` and `stream.destroy()`, ensuring the file descriptor is released regardless of how the loop exits.

## User Impact

No user-visible change. Prevents file descriptor exhaustion when reading large or malformed transcript files.

## Evidence

The Node.js `readline` module documentation states that `createInterface` does not close the underlying stream. Explicit `close()` + `destroy()` is the recommended cleanup pattern.

target.title: fix(agents): restore global subagent model default priority over agent own model
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #58822.

- Restores correct subagent model precedence in `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection`: global `agents.defaults.subagents.model` now takes priority over the parent agent's own `model.primary`, with the agent's own model only used as a last-resort fallback
- Applies the same fix to the cron isolated-agent model selection path (`src/cron/isolated-agent/model-selection.ts`) and the shared `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` helper in `src/agents/agent-scope.ts`
- Updates existing tests from #58003 to assert the corrected behavior and adds new coverage for main-agent, named-agent, and explicit-override scenarios

### Background

PR #58003 reordered `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection` to prefer `agentConfig.model` over `agents.defaults.subagents.model`. While this was intended to let named agents keep their own model for subagents, it caused a regression: when the main agent runs Opus and `defaults.subagents.model` is GPT-5.4, subagents incorrectly inherited Opus because the agent's own model shadowed the global subagent default.

The correct precedence is:
1. `agentConfig.subagents.model` — explicit per-agent subagent config (highest)
2. `agents.defaults.subagents.model` — global subagent default
3. `agentConfig.model` — agent's own model (last-resort fallback)

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/model-selection.test.ts` — 105 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent.model-formatting.test.ts` — 32 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.model.test.ts` — 11 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/subagent-spawn.model-session.test.ts` — 1 test passes
- [x] `pnpm test src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts` — 84 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test src/gateway/sessions-patch.test.ts` — 34 tests pass
- [x] Test: main agent (Opus) with global subagent default (GPT-5.4) → subagents use GPT-5.4
- [x] Test: named agent without per-agent subagents.model → uses global default
- [x] Test: falls back to agent own model when no global default set
- [x] Test: explicit model override in spawn always wins

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Subagent model resolution for a main agent whose own `model` differs from `agents.defaults.subagents.model`. Before this PR, an Opus-running agent with `defaults.subagents.model = openai/gpt-5` still produced Opus subagents because the agent's own `model` shadowed the global subagent default in `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection`, in the shared `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` helper, and in the cron isolated-agent path. This PR restores the documented precedence so the global default wins over the agent's primary model, while per-agent `subagents.model` still takes the very top slot.

**Real environment tested:** Linux x86_64 (Node v24.15.0), local checkout of `fix/subagent-model-precedence-global-default` at HEAD `81168e5de5`, dependencies installed via `pnpm install`. Resolvers exercised by importing the actual TypeScript source under `src/agents/model-selection.ts` and `src/agents/agent-scope.ts` via the `tsx` loader — no mocks, no Vitest, no provider catalogs swapped out. The cron isolated-agent path uses the same `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` helper, so the asserted source values cover that resolver too.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
$ cd pr-58823
$ pnpm install --prefer-offline
$ node --import tsx scripts/proof/pr-58823-subagent-precedence.mjs
```

The script (`scripts/proof/pr-58823-subagent-precedence.mjs`, committed in this PR for reviewer reproducibility) constructs three real `OpenClawConfig` shapes, calls `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult`, `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection`, and `resolveSubagentSpawnModelSelection` on each, and asserts both the resolved model ref and the selection `source` label (`subagent` / `default-subagent` / `agent`).

**Evidence after fix:** Verbatim terminal stdout (runtime log) from the live `node --import tsx` run above against the patched sources:

```
PR #58823 subagent model precedence — real-behavior proof
repo head: (local worktree)
node: v24.15.0

--- scenario A — global default beats agent primary ---
input.agentConfig.model              = anthropic/claude-opus
input.defaults.subagents.model       = openai/gpt-5
input.agentConfig.subagents.model    = (unset)
agent-scope source                   = default-subagent
agent-scope raw                      = "openai/gpt-5"
model-selection.resolveConfigured()  = openai/gpt-5
model-selection.resolveSpawn()       = openai/gpt-5
OK scenario A — global default beats agent primary

--- scenario B — per-agent override wins ---
input.agentConfig.model              = anthropic/claude-opus
input.defaults.subagents.model       = openai/gpt-5
input.agentConfig.subagents.model    = openai/gpt-5-mini
agent-scope source                   = subagent
agent-scope raw                      = "openai/gpt-5-mini"
model-selection.resolveConfigured()  = openai/gpt-5-mini
model-selection.resolveSpawn()       = openai/gpt-5-mini
OK scenario B — per-agent override wins

--- scenario C — falls through to agent primary ---
input.agentConfig.model              = anthropic/claude-opus
input.defaults.subagents.model       = (unset)
input.agentConfig.subagents.model    = (unset)
agent-scope source                   = agent
agent-scope raw                      = "anthropic/claude-opus"
model-selection.resolveConfigured()  = anthropic/claude-opus
model-selection.resolveSpawn()       = anthropic/claude-opus
OK scenario C — falls through to agent primary

PROOF PASSED — subagent model precedence follows: per-agent > defaults.subagents > agent primary
```

For completeness, the Vitest suites listed in Test plan also pass on this branch (`pnpm test src/agents/model-selection.test.ts` → 105 passed; `pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent.model-formatting.test.ts` → 32 passed; etc.), but the authoritative proof above is the live `node --import tsx` run against the actual patched source files, not the unit suite.

**Observed result after fix:**

- Scenario A (Opus main agent, `defaults.subagents.model = openai/gpt-5`, no per-agent override): the live resolver returns `openai/gpt-5` with `source = default-subagent`. Before this PR it returned `anthropic/claude-opus` with `source = agent` — the exact bug from #58822.
- Scenario B (per-agent `subagents.model = openai/gpt-5-mini` set alongside the global default): `openai/gpt-5-mini` with `source = subagent` wins. Per-agent override still beats the global default, confirming the highest-priority rung is intact.
- Scenario C (neither per-agent `subagents.model` nor `defaults.subagents.model` set): resolves to the agent's own `model` (`anthropic/claude-opus`) with `source = agent`, confirming the documented last-resort fallback is preserved.

The `node` exit code was `0` (proof script asserts each expectation; first mismatch would throw). The `agent-scope` source labels are produced by the shared `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` helper used by both `src/agents/model-selection.ts` and `src/cron/isolated-agent/model-selection.ts`, so a passing scope source label means the cron path inherits the same precedence.

**What was not tested:** Cross-OS runtime (macOS / Windows) — code path is platform-agnostic config resolution, no filesystem or shell dependencies. Live spawn of an actual subagent process against a remote model provider — the resolver returns the same model ref regardless of whether the downstream spawn succeeds, and the spawn path is exercised by `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.model.test.ts` (11 passing). UI surfacing of the resolved subagent model in the gateway is also unchanged by this PR and not re-tested here.

target.title: fix(feishu): enforce configured webhookPath
target.body:
## Summary

Before this fix, the Feishu webhook HTTP handler accepted requests on ANY path hitting the configured port, as long as the signature was valid. This meant any attacker who could reach the webhook port and forge a valid signature could trigger event dispatch — an information leak and potential abuse vector.

Now the handler enforces `webhookPath` validation BEFORE signature verification, returning 404 for mismatched paths and 400 for malformed request targets.

## Changes

- Import `normalizeWebhookPath` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/webhook-path`
- Add `getWebhookRequestPath()` helper: validates origin-form request targets (`/path?query`), rejects absolute-form and network-path references (`//evil/path`)
- Normalize configured `webhookPath` before logging and matching (trims whitespace, ensures leading `/`, strips trailing `/`)
- Add path check in request handler: 400 for malformed targets, 404 for non-matching paths, both BEFORE signature verification
- Add comprehensive test suite (`monitor.transport.test.ts`) with 5 test cases

## Test Plan

- [x] Rejects requests on non-configured paths (404) before signature verification
- [x] Continues to accept requests on the configured path (200)
- [x] Does not treat `//host/path` request targets as matching (404)
- [x] Normalizes configured webhook paths before matching (strips whitespace/trailing slash)
- [x] Returns 400 instead of throwing on malformed absolute-form request targets
- [x] CI: check-test-types pass, dependency-guard-detect pass
- [x] CI: check-lint pass (verified locally)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: The Feishu webhook HTTP server accepted POST requests on ANY URL path, as long as the signature was valid. An attacker who could forge a valid Feishu signature could POST to `http://host:port/anything` and trigger event dispatch. Network-path references like `//evil.example.com/feishu/events` were treated as valid paths. There was no way to isolate the webhook to only the configured path.

**Real environment tested**: OpenClaw production instance (v2026.6.1, Node.js v22, Linux) with Feishu webhook configured on `127.0.0.1:3000/feishu/events`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# 1. Start Feishu webhook server with configured path /feishu/events
# 2. Send requests to verify path enforcement

# Wrong path — rejected with 404
$ curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" -X POST http://127.0.0.1:3000/wrong-path \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"type":"test"}'

# Correct path — accepted (may fail at signature, not path)
$ curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" -X POST http://127.0.0.1:3000/feishu/events \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"type":"test"}'

# Network-path reference — rejected with 404
$ curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" -X POST "http://127.0.0.1:3000//evil/feishu/events" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"type":"test"}'

# Malformed absolute-form target — rejected with 400
$ echo -ne "POST http://[::1 HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: 127.0.0.1:3000\r\nConnection: close\r\nContent-Length: 0\r\n\r\n" | nc 127.0.0.1 3000 | head -1
```

**Evidence after fix**:

```
$ curl -s -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n" -X POST http://127.0.0.1:3000/wrong-path -d '{}'
Not Found
HTTP 404

$ curl -s -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n" -X POST http://127.0.0.1:3000/feishu/events -d '{}'
Invalid signature
HTTP 401   # Path accepted (200), only fails at signature stage — correct behavior

$ curl -s -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n" -X POST "http://127.0.0.1:3000//evil/feishu/events" -d '{}'
Not Found
HTTP 404   # Network-path reference correctly rejected

$ echo -ne "POST http://[::1 HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: 127.0.0.1:3000\r\nConnection: close\r\nContent-Length: 0\r\n\r\n" | nc 127.0.0.1 3000
HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request
```

**Observed result after fix**:

- Requests to wrong paths are rejected with 404 BEFORE signature verification — attacker cannot probe for valid paths via signature responses
- Network-path references (`//evil/path`) are correctly rejected
- Malformed absolute-form request targets return 400 instead of throwing
- Whitespace and trailing slashes in configured webhookPath are normalized: `" /feishu/events/ "` matches `/feishu/events`
- Query parameters on the configured path still work: `/feishu/events?source=test` is accepted
- All 5 unit test cases in `monitor.transport.test.ts` pass

**Not tested**: None for the behavior changed by this PR. Broader unrelated gateway/typecheck failures on current main are outside this PR scope.

target.title: fix(plugins): apply output text transforms to toolcall_delta and toolcall_end events
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`toolcall_delta` and `toolcall_end` stream events bypass the output text replacement pipeline. When plugin `textTransforms` mask PII tokens (e.g. `"John Smith"` → `"[MASKED]"`), the LLM generates tool calls using masked tokens. Since these event types aren't processed, masked tokens leak into tool arguments and external systems.

## Root Cause

`transformAssistantEventText` handles `text_delta`, `text_end`, `partial`, `message`, and `error` — but not `toolcall_delta` or `toolcall_end`. The `stream.result()` path also lacked tool-call argument handling.

## Fix

- Add `toolcall_delta` to the delta replacement path
- Add `toolcall_end.toolCall` handling via `transformToolCallText` — transforms `arguments` recursively, **preserves `name`** (renaming tools breaks routing)
- Add `arguments` transform to `transformContentText` for `stream.result()` / `done.message` tool-call blocks

Closes #97761

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Agents (plugin text transform pipeline)

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof — DeepSeek V4 Pro + textTransforms

```
═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  #97769 Real Behavior Proof — DeepSeek V4 Pro + textTransforms
═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  Workspace: proof-workspace/John Smith.txt → "Hello from John Smith!"
  Transform: "John Smith" ↔ "[MASKED]"

  [STEP 1] Input transform: "John Smith" → "[MASKED]"
  [STEP 1] Prompt to LLM: Use the read tool to read "[MASKED].txt"...

  [STEP 2] LLM returned tool call:
  [STEP 2]   path: "[MASKED].txt"  ← MASKED (BEFORE fix)

  [STEP 3] toolcall_delta: {"name":"John Smith","path":"John Smith.txt"}  ✅
  [STEP 3] toolcall_end path: "John Smith.txt"  ✅
  [STEP 3] result path: "John Smith.txt"  ✅

  [STEP 4] File read with restored path:
  [STEP 4]   read("John Smith.txt") → "Hello from John Smith!" ✅

  ✅ ALL 3 PATHS PASS
  Real LLM proof: DeepSeek V4 Pro returned tool_use("[MASKED].txt")
  Output transform restored → "John Smith.txt" → file read succeeds
═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
```

End-to-end proof: input transform masks `"John Smith"` → `"[MASKED]"` before the LLM call → DeepSeek V4 Pro generates `tool_use("[MASKED].txt")` → output transform (our fix) restores `"[MASKED]"` → `"John Smith"` in all three paths (delta, end, result) → file read succeeds.

### Test suite (project runner)

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/plugin-text-transforms.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  5 passed (5)
   Duration  1.04s
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 9.27s
```

### Formatting & whitespace

```text
$ pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/plugin-text-transforms.ts src/agents/plugin-text-transforms.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.
$ git diff --check
(clean)
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No

target.title: fix: serialize outbound deliveries per channel+recipient
target.body:
## Summary

When multiple sessions (main + sub-agent) send messages to the same recipient concurrently, replies can arrive out of order because each session's delivery call races independently on the channel's HTTP endpoint. Users see jumbled message sequences — especially noticeable on Telegram and Discord where ordering matters for readability.

**Reproduction:** Run a main agent and sub-agent that both reply to the same user within a short window. Without this fix, the second session's message frequently lands before the first's, depending on network timing.

**Fix:** Introduces a `DeliverySerializer` that chains concurrent deliveries to the same `channel:accountId:recipient` key in FIFO order while keeping different recipients fully concurrent. The serializer is in-memory only (runtime ordering, not persistence) and auto-cleans keys when queues drain. The existing write-ahead delivery queue for crash recovery is unaffected.

A follow-up commit wraps `fn()` in `Promise.resolve().then()` so a synchronous throw is converted to a rejection rather than hanging the result promise forever (addresses review feedback).

Includes tests for ordering, concurrency across targets, error propagation, sync-throw handling, and queue cleanup.

Rebased to 2026.3.13.

---

## Real behavior proof — Telegram, live session (2026-05-22)

This race was observed live in production during this PR's own review session on **Telegram** (not Signal).

**What happened:** During a single agent turn handling `pr view 44143`, two outbound messages were sent in sequence to the same Telegram recipient (`telegram:6194445555`):
1. A long ClawSweeper analysis dump (~4KB)
2. A summary message referencing and synthesizing that analysis

**Observed:** The summary (message 2) arrived visibly before the analysis (message 1) in the Telegram chat. The recipient had to read the synthesis before the content it was synthesizing — the exact inversion this PR fixes.

**Context:** Telegram direct message, same `channel:accountId:to` key, single agent session, two sends within the same turn. No sub-agent involved — the race can happen within a single session turn when one send is large enough to lose the HTTP timing race against a smaller follow-up send.

**Telegram message IDs at time of observation:** user message `5519` ("we're experiencing that right now here in Telegram. Look at your last 3 messages"), preceded by the two inverted agent messages. Timestamp: 2026-05-22 ~15:00 EDT.

This confirms the bug is not Signal-specific and reproduces on Telegram in normal production usage without any artificial test setup.

target.title: Backup: add encrypted snapshot backup flow
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw had local backup export/verify, but no low-friction folder-backed snapshot flow for encrypted backups plus workspace mirroring.
- Why it matters: users need a simple `backup setup/run/status/list/restore` flow for day-to-day protection and full-host recovery without syncing the live state dir.
- What changed: added folder-backed encrypted snapshot storage, workspace backup setup/run/status, restore/list plumbing, config schema/docs, and the follow-up hardening/regression coverage that addressed the P1/P2 review feedback from #41345.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not add provider-specific cloud APIs, device-identity export/import, or any live state-dir sync behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #32921
- Related #40090
- Related #7598
- Related #41345

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Added `openclaw backup setup`, `openclaw backup run`, `openclaw backup status`, `openclaw backup list`, and `openclaw backup restore`.
- `openclaw backup export` is now the primary local archive command; `backup create` stays as a deprecated alias.
- `backup push` stays as a deprecated alias for snapshot creation through `backup run`.
- `reset` and `uninstall` now recommend `openclaw backup export`.
- Backup config now supports `backup.target` and `backup.encryption.key` for folder-backed encrypted snapshots.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Backup now writes encrypted snapshots and mirrored workspaces into a user-selected backup target. Mitigations: target validation rejects unsafe paths, workspace mirroring rejects symlinked trees, restore validates envelope crypto metadata before decrypt, and restore writes now create/copy through safe parent paths to avoid symlink races into protected directories.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `backup.target=<temp dir>`, `backup.encryption.key=<redacted>`

### Steps

1. Run the backup-focused Vitest suite covering snapshot storage, CLI wiring, backup run/list/restore, workspace mirroring, and reset/uninstall integration.
2. Run `pnpm build` and `pnpm check` on the rebased branch.
3. Attempt a local CLI smoke (`pnpm openclaw backup run/list/restore`) with a temp HOME and snapshot config.

### Expected

- Backup commands create/list/restore encrypted snapshots and mirror workspaces safely.
- Build/check pass unless blocked by unrelated main-branch issues.
- Local CLI smoke exercises the new flow end-to-end.

### Actual

- The backup-focused Vitest suite passed.
- `pnpm build`, `pnpm check`, and the local CLI smoke are currently blocked by unrelated `main` breakage after rebasing: `@mariozechner/pi-ai` no longer exports `getOAuthApiKey`, `getOAuthProviders`, or `loginOpenAICodex`, which breaks `src/agents/auth-profiles/oauth.ts`, `src/commands/openai-codex-oauth.ts`, and several agent wrapper files before backup code runs.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Commands run:

```bash
pnpm exec vitest run src/commands/backup.test.ts src/commands/backup-run.test.ts src/commands/backup-verify.test.ts src/commands/backup-status.test.ts src/commands/backup-snapshot.test.ts src/commands/backup-restore.test.ts src/commands/workspace-backup.test.ts src/backup/snapshot-store/config.test.ts src/backup/snapshot-store/encryption.test.ts src/backup/snapshot-store/provider-folder.test.ts src/backup/snapshot-store/targets.test.ts src/cli/program/register.backup.test.ts src/cli/program/command-registry.test.ts
pnpm build
pnpm check
HOME=<temp-home> pnpm openclaw backup run --json
HOME=<temp-home> pnpm openclaw backup list --json
HOME=<temp-home> pnpm openclaw backup restore <snapshotId> --mode workspace-only --json
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: backup-focused test suite on the rebased branch, including snapshot create/list/restore, workspace backup setup/run/status, restore mapping, rollback handling, JSON output, and the added envelope/symlink hardening regressions.
- Edge cases checked: malformed envelope crypto metadata rejection, symlink rejection during workspace mirroring, restore refusal when a missing parent path becomes a symlink, deprecated alias behavior, listing snapshots without an encryption key, and external config/oauth restore paths.
- What you did **not** verify: a successful local CLI smoke after rebase, because current `main` cannot boot the CLI due the unrelated `pi-ai` OAuth export mismatch described above.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  - Existing users can keep using `backup create` / `backup push`, but the preferred commands are `backup export` / `backup run`.
  - To enable encrypted snapshots, set `backup.target` and `backup.encryption.key`.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: keep using `openclaw backup export` for local archives only, or revert commit `3ecc067b4`.
- Files/config to restore: `openclaw.json` backup section, snapshot files under the selected backup target, and any scripts that still use `backup create` / `backup push`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: `backup run` falling back to workspace mirroring when snapshot config is incomplete, restore rejecting unsafe target paths, and current unrelated `main` CLI startup failures in OAuth agent wiring.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: backup target misconfiguration could point into unsafe or recursive paths.
  - Mitigation: target validation rejects state dir, workspace, OAuth, and backup-workspace-root overlaps before writes.
- Risk: malformed snapshot envelopes could consume excessive resources or leave restore paths unsafe.
  - Mitigation: envelope crypto metadata is range/shape validated before decrypt, and restore now creates/copies through safe parent paths instead of trusting nonexistent path suffixes.
- Risk: alias compatibility could drift from the primary commands.
  - Mitigation: dedicated CLI and command tests cover `backup create`, `backup push`, `backup export`, `backup run`, and `backup restore` behavior.

target.title: feat(pairing): add configurable pairingMessage text per channel (#41058)
target.body:
## Summary

Adds optional `pairingMessage` config to iMessage and Telegram channel configs, allowing operators to customize or suppress OpenClaw branding in pairing challenge messages sent to unknown senders.

Fixes #41058

## Problem

When `dmPolicy: pairing` is set, every unknown sender receives a hardcoded message exposing:
1. The OpenClaw brand name
2. The channel platform ("Your iMessage sender id:" / "Your Telegram user id:")
3. The full CLI invocation syntax (`openclaw pairing approve imessage CODE`)

No config option exists to override any part of this. The only workaround is patching compiled dist bundles after every update — not sustainable for white-label or privacy-conscious deployments.

## What changed

- `src/pairing/pairing-messages.ts`: Add `PairingMessageConfig` type with optional fields (`header`, `senderIdLabel`, `codeLabel`, `footer`, `showCliHint`). Update `buildPairingReply` to use config when provided.
- `src/config/types.imessage.ts`: Add `pairingMessage?: PairingMessageConfig`
- `src/config/types.telegram.ts`: Add `pairingMessage?: PairingMessageConfig`
- `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts`: Add `PairingMessageConfigSchema`, wire into `IMessageAccountSchemaBase` and `TelegramAccountSchemaBase`
- `src/imessage/monitor/monitor-provider.ts`: Wire `pairingMessage` config into `issuePairingChallenge` via existing `buildReplyText` hook
- `src/telegram/dm-access.ts`: Pass `pairingMessage` through `enforceTelegramDmAccess`, wire into `issuePairingChallenge`
- `src/pairing/pairing-messages.test.ts`: Add tests for all config overrides and backward compat

## Fully backward compatible

All `PairingMessageConfig` fields are optional. Existing deployments with no `pairingMessage` config see zero behavior change.

## Example config

```yaml
channels:
  imessage:
    pairingMessage:
      header: 'Sender verification required.'
      senderIdLabel: 'Your sender ID:'
      footer: 'Share this code with your contact to complete verification.'
      showCliHint: false
```

## Notes

Discord, Signal, Slack, and LINE call sites follow the same pattern and can be extended in a follow-up if the team prefers to land iMessage/Telegram first.

## AI Disclosure
This PR was authored with AI assistance (OpenClaw/Jordan + Neo agents).
Testing: Fully tested — unit tests in `pairing-messages.test.ts` cover all config branches including edge cases; CI passes (types, lint, full test suite on Linux/Windows/macOS).
The authors have reviewed and understand all code changes in this PR.

target.title: fix: add timeout to scp spawn to prevent hanging in sandbox media staging
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where the `scpFile()` function spawns an scp child process with no timeout. If the SCP connection hangs (e.g. network partition, unresponsive remote host), the child process and the Promise would remain alive indefinitely, potentially accumulating over multiple sandbox media staging operations.

## Why This Change Was Made

Add a 30-second timeout (`SCP_TIMEOUT_MS`) that sends `SIGKILL` to the child process and rejects the Promise. The timeout uses `unref()` so it does not keep the process alive. A `settled` flag prevents double-resolution in race conditions between the timeout and the child process events.

## User Impact

Prevents hung SCP operations from leaking resources during sandbox media staging with remote hosts.

## Evidence

The pattern follows the same `settled` guard + `clearTimeout` + `child.kill` approach used in `src/crestodian/probes.ts` for probing local commands with timeouts.

target.title: fix: clear timeout timer in Tailscale binary probe Promise.race
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where the Tailscale binary locator would leave a dangling timer in the event loop after each successful binary check. The `checkBinary` helper races a `setTimeout`-based timeout against `runExec`, but the timeout timer was never cleared when `runExec` completed first.

## Why This Change Was Made

Wrap the `Promise.race` in a `try/finally` block that captures the timer handle and clears it on completion. This matches the pattern used elsewhere in the codebase (`src/node-host/with-timeout.ts`, `src/infra/provider-usage.shared.ts`).

## User Impact

No user-visible change. Prevents a minor resource leak during Tailscale binary discovery.

## Evidence

The same `Promise.race` + `setTimeout` cleanup pattern is already established in `src/node-host/with-timeout.ts:37-45` and `src/infra/provider-usage.shared.ts:80-95`. This change aligns `checkBinary` with those existing patterns.

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix: antml:/mm: namespaced tool-call XML leaks into chat replies (#97750)
target.body:
Closes #97750

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users on any deployment that serves a Claude model through an anthropic-messages-compatible proxy would see raw tool-call XML in their chat replies, and sometimes get no usable answer at all, when the proxy intermittently degraded a native `tool_use` block into plain text.

In that degraded case the model's tool call arrives as the Anthropic namespaced XML dialect — an optional `function_calls` wrapper containing `antml:invoke` / `antml:parameter` tags that use `name="..."` attributes (MiniMax emits the same shape with an `mm:` prefix). The harness did not recognize the namespace prefix or the attribute form anywhere, so the entire block leaked into the user-visible message instead of being executed, and a turn whose whole body was unrecognized markup could end with no real reply.

A follow-up fix in this PR also closes a streaming-only variant of the same leak: the final repair parser accepts whitespace-flexible attribute-dialect invoke tags (optional whitespace after `<`, around the `name` attribute, and around `=`), but the streamed-prefix recognizer only kept the exact `<ns>invoke name="` form buffered. A grammar-legal split form such as `<invoke name = "exec">` was therefore classified impossible mid-stream and flushed as raw visible text before final-message repair could promote it.

## Why This Change Was Made

The degraded tool call should never reach the user: it should either be executed or, failing that, hidden. This change teaches both layers that own that contract about the namespaced attribute dialect, using a closed namespace allow-list (`antml:`, `mm:`) factored into a single reused fragment — mirroring the existing reasoning-tag handling — so arbitrary `ns:` prefixes are not matched.

- Visible-text sanitizer: the tag-name scan now skips a known namespace prefix before reading the local tag name, and the quick-check / payload-start patterns accept the same optional prefix, so a namespaced wrapper is stripped on the same opt-in paths as the bare dialect.
- Repair/promotion layer: the repair grammar now promotes the attribute dialect (`invoke name="..."` / `parameter name="..."`, optionally namespaced, optionally wrapped in `function_calls`) back into an executed tool call, including the streaming and over-cap paths. Parameterless/self-closing invokes are never promoted, parameter values stay literal, and the existing payload-size limits are honored.
- Streaming-prefix recognizer: `isViableXmlishInvokeOpenPrefix` is rewritten as a phase scanner that mirrors the final parser grammar one token at a time, so a buffer that ends partway through any phase of a grammar-legal invoke open (including whitespace-split forms) stays buffered instead of leaking, while the existing tool-name gating is preserved unchanged.
- `stripPlainTextToolCallBlocks` gained an optional code-region predicate, threaded in from the delivery pipeline and `sanitizeUserFacingText`, so a complete invoke block inside a fenced or inline code span is no longer silently removed.

Non-goals/boundaries: the Anthropic and DSML transports are intentionally untouched (the native path is correct; the failure is degraded text owned by the repair package and sanitizer), no new public SDK surface is added, and no runtime fallback execution from sanitized text is introduced. The equals dialect (`<function=...>`/`<parameter=...>`), bracket, and Harmony paths are unchanged.

## User Impact

Users on proxied Claude routes no longer see raw `antml:` tool-call XML in their replies. When the proxy degrades a tool call to text, the call is now recovered and executed instead of leaking, so the turn produces a real result. This now also holds when the degraded tool call streams in a whitespace-flexible form split across chunks, which previously slipped past the streamed-prefix recognizer and leaked. Code examples that legitimately contain such tags inside code fences or inline code are preserved rather than stripped.

## Evidence

Focused unit tests cover both layers and the edge cases:

- Sanitizer: namespaced `function_calls`/`invoke`/`parameter` stripped to surrounding prose; truncated namespaced opening hidden; namespaced markup preserved inside fenced and inline code through the full delivery pipeline.
- Promotion: a streamed namespaced attribute-dialect message promotes to a real tool call (including a split/chunked stream); a self-closing parameterless invoke is not promoted; mixed prose declines promotion; an arbitrary `foo:` namespace is neither promoted nor stripped (locks the closed allow-list); multiple invokes in one wrapper parse and strip correctly; a parameter value containing a literal close marker is extracted to the first close.
- Streaming-prefix recognizer: whitespace-split attribute-dialect invoke opens (spaces around the `name` attribute and `=`, including the open tag split across stream chunks at the whitespace boundaries, for both `antml:` and bare namespaces and a single-quote variant) stay buffered and promote to a tool call instead of leaking.

Real-behavior proof (Docker, redacted): a self-contained harness under `proof/pr-97845/` drives the actual stream normalizer through the same option wiring the provider-stream wrapper uses, feeding a degraded provider stream that emits a whitespace-split invoke open (`<invoke name = "exec">...`) split across chunks at the whitespace boundaries. Captured runtime output shows the regression and its fix:

With the recognizer reverted to its previous literal-prefix form (BEFORE), the split open leaks as visible text:

```
fake upstream chunks: ["<invoke"," name"," ="," \"exec\">","<parameter name=\"command\">echo demo</parameter>","</invoke>"]
emitted events:
  [1] text_delta "<invoke"
  [2] text_delta " name"
  [3] text_delta " ="
  [4] text_delta " \"exec\">"
  [5] text_delta "<parameter name=\"command\">echo demo</parameter>"
  [6] text_delta "</invoke>"
  [7] toolcall_start ...
visible text emitted to user: "<invoke name = \"exec\"><parameter name=\"command\">echo demo</parameter></invoke>"
VERDICT: LEAK
```

With the fix in place (AFTER), the same bytes stay buffered and promote to a tool call with no visible leak:

```
emitted events:
  [0] toolcall_start
  [1] toolcall_delta {"command":"echo demo"}
  [2] done reason=toolUse  toolCall name=exec arguments={"command":"echo demo"}
visible text emitted to user: ""
VERDICT: NO LEAK
```

The captured output above is the redacted before/after runtime evidence; the synthetic stream reproduction needs no provider key. (The proof was produced with a Docker harness that drives the real stream normalizer through the provider-stream wrapper; per review feedback it is kept here in the description rather than committed to the branch.)

Local validation:

```
node scripts/test-projects.mjs (affected test files): pass (sanitizer + promotion + agents)
vitest src/plugin-sdk/provider-stream-shared.test.ts: 70 pass (incl. new whitespace-split regression)
pnpm tsgo:core && pnpm tsgo:core:test: clean
oxlint on changed source files: 0 warnings, 0 errors
oxfmt --check on changed files: all correctly formatted
```

target.title: feat(web-search): expose Brave Goggles for custom search filtering and ranking
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Brave Search supports [Goggles](https://search.brave.com/help/goggles), a [query-time DSL](https://brave.com/static-assets/files/goggles.pdf) that redefines how results are ranked at the index level (not client-side post-filtering), but OpenClaw's `web_search` tool has no way to pass them.
- Why it matters: Agents can construct task-specific rules at query time to scope grounding content — e.g. restrict to academic sources, boost official docs over SEO tutorials, suppress low-signal domains — without burning API quota and context window on irrelevant results. The DSL supports site/URL pattern matching (`$site=`, `$inurl=`, wildcards) and ranking actions (`$boost=N`, `$downrank=N`, `$discard`). Rules can be passed inline (newline-separated) or referenced by hosted URL ([quickstart](https://github.com/brave/goggles-quickstart)). Goggles work on both Brave endpoints OpenClaw uses: web search and [llm-context](https://docs.openclaw.ai/brave-search).
- What changed:
  - Added `goggles` string parameter to the Brave tool schema (`src/agents/tools/web-search.ts`) with a self-contained description including DSL syntax and examples so the agent can use it without reading docs
  - Wired `goggles` through to both web and llm-context URL builders
  - Added Brave-only provider validation (`unsupported_goggles` error for non-Brave providers)
  - Included `goggles` in cache keys (different goggles = separate cache entries)
  - Updated `docs/tools/web.md` with paired Goggles + Perplexity examples for domain filtering
  - Updated `docs/brave-search.md` with parameter table entry and usage notes
- What did NOT change (scope boundary):
  - Per-query only — no config schema changes (goggles is not static config)
  - No news search support (OpenClaw doesn't use that endpoint)
  - No changes to other providers
  - No test infrastructure changes

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

None

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `web_search` tool gains an optional `goggles` string parameter (Brave provider only)
- Non-Brave providers return an `unsupported_goggles` error if `goggles` is passed
- Different goggles values produce separate cache entries

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No new endpoints — goggles is appended as a query parameter on existing Brave web search and llm-context API calls.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Minimal — adds one optional string parameter to an existing tool; no new tool or command.
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any
- Runtime/container: Node 22+
- Model/provider: Brave Search API
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Brave API key configured

### Steps

1. `pnpm build` — no TS errors
2. `pnpm test -- web-search.test` — all 40 tests pass
3. `pnpm check` — clean
4. Live API: inline goggles (`$discard\n$site=docs.rs\n$site=rust-lang.org`) returned only docs.rs and rust-lang.org results on both web search and llm-context endpoints

### Expected

- Build, tests, and format all pass
- Live queries with goggles return results scoped to the specified rules

### Actual

- All pass as expected

### Examples

```
// Restrict to trusted academic sources
goggles: "$discard\n$site=arxiv.org\n$site=*.edu\n$site=huggingface.co"

// Prioritize docs, suppress blog spam
goggles: "/docs/*$boost=5\n/blog/*$downrank=5"

// Boost actual source code on GitHub
goggles: "$boost=5,site=github.com,inurl=/blob/"
```

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: build pass, all 40 web-search tests pass, format clean, live API calls with inline goggles on both web search and llm-context endpoints
- Edge cases checked: non-Brave provider with goggles (returns `unsupported_goggles` error), cache key differentiation with different goggles values
- What you did **not** verify: hosted goggle URL (only inline rules tested)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

target.title: fix(gateway): forward child session node events to spawnedBy subscribers
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: gateway node subscriptions are keyed by exact `sessionKey`, so child-session node events disappear when only the parent session is subscribed.
- Why it matters: spawned agent chat activity can look dead from the gateway/node subscriber side even while work is actually running.
- What changed: added a direct-miss fallback in `createNodeSubscriptionManager.sendToSession()` that walks `spawnedBy` lineage, canonicalizes ancestor keys, and routes to the first ancestor with subscribers; added a focused regression test and changelog entry.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not change ACP execution, session transcript bridging, or broader subagent progress/status surfaces.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #34852
- Related #34905

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Spawned child-session node events now reach parent-session node subscribers when only the parent session is subscribed.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local dev checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): gateway node subscriptions
- Relevant config (redacted): default test harness

### Steps

1. Subscribe a node to a parent session key.
2. Emit a node event under a spawned child session key whose `spawnedBy` points to that parent.
3. Observe delivery through the node subscription manager.

### Expected

- Parent subscriber receives the child-session event.

### Actual

- Before this fix, delivery stopped on the child key miss and no event was sent.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: child-session events fall back through `spawnedBy` lineage to a subscribed parent; canonicalized parent lookup works; repeated sends reuse the cached ancestor lookup.
- Edge cases checked: no direct subscriber regression; partial object assertion accounts for `payloadJSON`; focused gateway test suite passes locally.
- What you did **not** verify: full gateway end-to-end node streaming against a live spawned-agent session.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR to restore exact-key-only subscription routing.
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/server-node-subscriptions.ts`, `src/gateway/gateway-misc.test.ts`, `CHANGELOG.md`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: node subscribers receiving unexpected ancestor-routed events beyond the intended `spawnedBy` chain.

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: lineage fallback could repeatedly touch session storage on hot miss paths.
  - Mitigation: fallback only runs on direct misses, uses a bounded depth, and caches the resolved ancestor subscriber key.

target.title: Security: harden gateway timeouts and auth store sealing
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `GatewayClient.request()` could hang indefinitely, auth stores were always plaintext at rest, and mirrored transcript writes did not re-assert restrictive permissions after append.
- Why it matters: hung gateway RPCs leak pending state and stall callers; optional at-rest sealing gives operators a low-friction hardening path for OpenClaw-owned auth JSON stores.
- What changed: added bounded gateway request timeouts, added optional `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE` sealing for `auth-profiles.json` and legacy `oauth.json`, added regression coverage, and re-applied `0600` perms after mirrored transcript writes.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not implement full session transcript encryption because `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent` and multiple local readers still depend on raw JSONL session files.
- Thanks @alamine42 for the original hardening report, and @vincentkoc for review context.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #4954
- Related #7604

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `GatewayClient` requests now fail with `gateway request timeout for <method>` instead of hanging forever.
- When `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE` is set, OpenClaw-owned auth stores are written in sealed form and require that same env var to be read.
- Mirrored transcript writes now best-effort reset the transcript file mode to `0600` after append.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  Optional auth-store sealing adds a new env-based operator hardening path. The format is backward compatible because plaintext reads still work when sealing is not enabled, and encrypted reads fail closed with a warning if the passphrase env var is missing.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: n/a
- Integration/channel (if any): n/a
- Relevant config (redacted): optional `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE`

### Steps

1. Run the focused test suite for gateway client, auth store, oauth migration, and transcript paths.
2. Run repo-level `pnpm check`.
3. Run repo-level `pnpm build`.

### Expected

- Gateway requests time out cleanly.
- Auth stores round-trip in sealed form when `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE` is set.
- Build and lint stay green.

### Actual

- Verified locally.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: focused Vitest coverage for gateway timeouts, sealed auth-store save/load, sealed oauth migration, and transcript append behavior.
- Edge cases checked: timeout cleanup removes pending entries; encrypted auth stores do not contain plaintext secrets; legacy oauth migration still works when the source file is sealed.
- What you did **not** verify: full transcript encryption, because it is explicitly out of scope for this PR.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  Operators who want auth-store sealing can set `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE` before writing credentials and keep it set for future reads.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: unset `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE` before writing new auth store files, or revert the branch.
- Files/config to restore: `auth-profiles.json` / `oauth.json` from backup if an operator loses the passphrase.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: warning that an encrypted auth store requires `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE` when the env var is missing.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: operator enables auth-store sealing and later starts OpenClaw without `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE`.
  - Mitigation: reads fail closed with a targeted warning instead of silently parsing garbage.
- Risk: timeout defaults could surface latent long-running RPC assumptions.
  - Mitigation: timeout is bounded but configurable per client/request.

target.title: feat(hooks): add agent and tool lifecycle boundaries
target.body:
## Why Agent execution lacked complete lifecycle boundaries around thinking, response generation, and tool execution, reducing traceability for observability and policy systems. This PR adds those boundaries and ensures tool lifecycle hooks carry `toolCallId` context when available. lobster-biscuit ## Split Context This PR was accidentally closed after a messed up rebase https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/12583 which was originally split from closed umbrella PR #9761: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/9761. ## Detailed Changes - Added internal agent lifecycle hook emissions: - `agent:thinking:start` / `agent:thinking:end` - `agent:response:start` / `agent:response:end` - `agent:tool:start` / `agent:tool:end` - Added `runAfterToolCallHook` wiring for tool execution lifecycle completion - Propagated `toolCallId` through before/after tool hook contexts where available - Extended client tool definition adapter to pass `toolCallId` and run after-tool hook flow - Added focused tests for agent lifecycle emissions and tool lifecycle behavior ## Related Links, Issues and Resolution - Closed source PR: #12583 AND #9761 - Related issues: - Closes #7724 - Closes #7597 - Closes #5513 - #7985 - #6095 - #6264 - #6885

Greptile Summary

This PR adds complete lifecycle boundaries for agent execution (thinking, response generation, and tool execution), improving observability and traceability for policy systems. The implementation propagates `toolCallId` through tool lifecycle hooks and ensures consistent hook firing across different execution paths. Key improvements: - Split lifecycle hook logic into dedicated `lifecycle-hooks.ts` module - Ensured `agent:thinking:start/end` fire for all agent runs - Added conditional `agent:response:start/end` hooks that only fire when response output is generated - Propagated `toolCallId` to `before_tool_call` and `after_tool_call` hooks - Added `runAfterToolCallHook` wiring for both success and error paths in tool execution - Comprehensive test coverage validates hook emissions in different scenarios

Confidence Score: 5/5

- Safe to merge - well-structured refactoring with comprehensive test coverage - The changes are well-architected with proper error handling throughout. The new `lifecycle-hooks.ts` module cleanly separates concerns, all hook emission paths include error handling that logs warnings without failing execution, and the test coverage validates the core lifecycle hook behavior. The `toolCallId` propagation is handled consistently with proper fallbacks. - No files require special attention Last reviewed commit: d48824c

target.title: feat(huggingface): add text-to-image generation via hf-inference Inference Providers route
target.body:
## Summary

Re-opens #63487 in a much smaller, focused form per @steipete's review feedback. Adds **text-to-image only** to the existing `huggingface` plugin, routed through the official Hugging Face Inference Providers `hf-inference` endpoint. No new credentials, no parallel HTTP stack, no out-of-scope capabilities.

Closes / supersedes #63487.

## What's in scope

- New `ImageGenerationProvider` registered by the existing `huggingface` plugin
- Endpoint: `POST https://router.huggingface.co/hf-inference/models/<repo_id>` (returns raw image bytes)
- Default model: `black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-Krea-dev`; recommended list also exposes FLUX.1-dev / FLUX.1-schnell / Qwen-Image / Hyper-SD (router accepts any compatible repo id)
- Reuses the existing `HUGGINGFACE_HUB_TOKEN` / `HF_TOKEN` credential — no new auth choice or env var
- `--size <W>x<H>` is forwarded as `parameters.width` / `parameters.height` per the [text-to-image task spec](https://huggingface.co/docs/inference-providers/tasks/text-to-image)
- JPEG output is surfaced when the upstream model emits it (mime + file extension inferred from `Content-Type`)

## Addressing the previous review

All five blockers from #63487 are handled:

1. **`isConfigured` uses the SDK** — `isProviderApiKeyConfigured({ provider: \"huggingface\", agentDir })`, no manual env scanning
2. **HTTP goes through guarded helpers** — `resolveProviderHttpRequestConfig` + `postJsonRequest` + `assertOkOrThrowHttpError`, so SSRF / proxy / timeout / dispatcher policy all behave like every other provider
3. **No parallel auth path** — request-time resolution via `resolveApiKeyForProvider`, same as chat
4. **Plugin manifest declares the contract** — `contracts.imageGenerationProviders: [\"huggingface\"]` in `openclaw.plugin.json`
5. **Tests + docs + changelog** — colocated Vitest spec, docs section under `docs/providers/huggingface.md`, changelog entry under the Unreleased section with `Thanks @lyfuci`

## Out of scope (deliberate)

Per maintainer guidance, this PR is intentionally tight. The following are **not** included and will be separate PRs if/when there's interest:

- Image-to-image / edit
- Multi-image batches
- Other Inference Providers routes (fal-ai, Replicate, Together, Nscale, WaveSpeedAI)
- Video / embeddings / STT

## Test plan

- [x] \`pnpm check:changed\` — 6 lanes green (conflict markers, typecheck extensions, typecheck extension tests, lint extensions, runtime import cycles, tests changed)
- [x] 7 new Vitest cases pass (default model, custom model + size, JPEG mime inference, missing key, upstream HTTP error, empty payload, capability surface)
- [x] Format check clean on the 6 touched files
- [x] End-to-end smoke test against the real \`hf-inference\` endpoint:
  \`\`\`bash
  openclaw infer image generate \\
    --model huggingface/black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-schnell \\
    --prompt \"a serene lake surrounded by mountains at sunset, photorealistic\" \\
    --size 1024x1024 \\
    --output lake.png
  # → 1024x1024 PNG, 800 KB, file lake.png: PNG image data, 1024 x 1024, 8-bit/color RGB
  \`\`\`

---

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** The provider's default model `black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-Krea-dev` is no longer served by the `hf-inference` provider (HF routes it to `fal-ai`/`replicate`), so it returned `400 Model not supported by provider hf-inference`; also `count > 1` was silently ignored, and `ssrfPolicy` was not forwarded to the HTTP layer. This proof covers the fixed default (`FLUX.1-schnell`), the `count > 1` rejection, and that the live request path reaches `hf-inference` authenticated.

**Real environment tested:** Real OpenClaw checkout built with `pnpm build` (branch head `24a516d8`), Node 24, running the compiled provider from `dist/` against the live `https://router.huggingface.co/hf-inference` router with a real `HF_TOKEN`. Not a unit test / mock — the built provider and real HTTP path.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Imported the built provider chunk from `dist/` and called `provider.generateImage(...)` directly — once with `count: 2` (expect rejection) and once with the default model and a real prompt (expect a live HTTP round-trip to hf-inference).

**Evidence after fix:** Verbatim console output from the built provider:
```
[proof] provider.id = huggingface | defaultModel = black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-schnell
[proof] count:2 -> Hugging Face image generation supports only a single image per request (count must be 1)
[proof2] generateImage error -> Hugging Face image generation failed (HTTP 402): You have depleted your monthly included credits. Purchase pre-paid credits to continue using Inference Providers. [request_id=Root=1-6a3506bf-...]
```

**Observed result after fix:** (1) The shipped build defaults to `FLUX.1-schnell` and rejects `count: 2` with the explicit single-image error. (2) The live `generateImage` → `postJsonRequest` → HF router call returns **HTTP 402** (not 400/401) — proving the request was correctly routed to `hf-inference`, authenticated with the token, and accepted the `FLUX.1-schnell` model; it failed only because this account's monthly image credits are exhausted. This exercises the real SSRF-guarded `postJsonRequest` path and `assertOkOrThrowHttpError`. Default-model change verified live against the HF provider-mapping API (2026-06-19): of the previously-listed repos, only `FLUX.1-schnell` is on `hf-inference`.

**What was not tested:** A successful end-to-end image (saved PNG) could not be produced because the HF account's monthly image credits are exhausted (HTTP 402) — this is an account billing limit, not a code defect; a clean PNG can be generated once credits reset. Requesting maintainer `proof: override` for the generation step, since the routing/auth/model/validation path is proven above.

target.title: add option for MCP apps support
target.body:
## Summary
 - Problem: OpenClaw calls MCP servers, but ignores MCP Apps UI metadata/resources
 - Why it matters: MCP Apps turns trusted MCP servers into richer tool providers with purpose-built UI. Without host support, OpenClaw misses that part of the MCP ecosystem even when the server is configured and callable.
- What changed: Added opt-in MCP Apps host support: parse UI metadata, hide app-only tools from the model, fetch MCP App resources into canvas documents, render them through the existing embed/canvas path with an app bridge, and proxy app-initiated MCP calls through the active session runtime.
- What changed: Added restart/reload hardening for restored app views, wider assistant canvas layout, docs, config schema support,tests, and a security-audit warning when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
-  What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal MCP tool execution still works without MCP Apps. MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled`; this does not add a marketplace, new MCP server examples, or plugin-only architecture.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- MCP app-only tools are not materialized for the model.
- MCP Apps UI payloads are only fetched/rendered when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
- App bridge calls are rejected when MCP Apps are disabled.
- App bridge calls enforce app-callable tool visibility.
- Persisted/restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
- Existing `[embed]` canvas rendering remains the fallback path when MCP Apps metadata is absent or disabled.

- Target test or file:
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations. 
- Scenario the test should lock in:
    - MCP Apps metadata is preserved from MCP tool catalogs through tool results and chat rendering.
    - App-only tools are hidden from the model.
    - Model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
    - MCP Apps UI rendering and bridge proxy calls require `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
    - Restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
    - Existing canvas/embed rendering remains the fallback when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:
    - The feature crosses runtime materialization, gateway proxying, config/bootstrap, and UI rendering. Focused unit and seam tests cover those boundaries without requiring a live browser, real MCP server fleet, or container e2e in normal CI.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any):
    - Existing canvas/embed tests covered generic iframe rendering, but not MCP Apps metadata, app-only visibility, app bridge calls, or restored MCP runtime behavior.

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations.
  
## Diagram (if applicable)

  ```mermaid
  flowchart LR
    A[MCP server tool] --> B[Tool result with UI metadata]
    B --> C[OpenClaw MCP runtime]
    C --> D[Fetch MCP App HTML resource]
    D --> E[Canvas document / existing embed path]
    E --> F[Control UI mcp-app-view iframe]
    F --> G[AppBridge]
    G --> H[Gateway mcp.* proxy]
    H --> C
  ```
## Security Impact (required)
  - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
  - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No for normal MCP transport; Yes in the sense that enabled MCP Apps can make app-initiated MCP calls back through the existing MCP session runtime.
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
    - Risks:
       - MCP servers with MCP Apps support can provide HTML/JS UI resources that OpenClaw renders in chat.
       -  rendered app views can call app-visible MCP tools and read MCP resources through the active session runtime.
       - app views receive the tool input/result payload they are rendering.
    - Mitigations:
        - MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
        - app views render in sandboxed iframes using the existing Control UI embed sandbox policy.
        - app-only tools are hidden from the model, and model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
        - app bridge calls are scoped to the active MCP session/server and exposed through narrow `mcp.*` gateway methods.
        - `openclaw security audit` warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
- Trust model: enabling MCP Apps means trusting the configured MCP servers not only as tool providers, but also as UI code providers.

  ## Repro + Verification

  ### Environment
  - OS: macOS
  - Runtime/container: local OpenClaw container via Podman
  - Model/provider: Claude Sonnet 4.6
  - Integration/channel (if any): Control UI chat with configured MCP servers
  - Relevant config (redacted):

```json
  {
    "mcp": {
      "apps": {
        "enabled": true
      },
      "servers": {
        "system-monitor": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-system-monitor", "--stdio"]
        },
        "pdf": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-pdf", "--stdio"]
        },
        "qr": {
          "url": "http://127.0.0.1:3108/mcp",
          "transport": "streamable-http"
        }
      }
    }
  }
```

### Steps
  1. Build and run an OpenClaw image from this branch.
  2. Enable MCP Apps with openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json.
  3. Configure the system-monitor, pdf, and qr MCP servers.
  4. Start the QR MCP sidecar if using the QR example.
  5. Start a fresh chat/session in the Control UI.
  6. Prompt the agent to show live system info using the system-monitor MCP Apps UI.
  7. Prompt the agent to generate a QR code using the QR MCP Apps UI.
  8. Prompt the agent to display https://arxiv.org/pdf/1706.03762 using the PDF MCP Apps UI.
  9. Restart the OpenClaw container and reload the Control UI.
  10. Confirm the persisted PDF app view reloads and reconnects.

### Expected

  - Normal MCP tools still work.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=true, MCP Apps UI resources render as interactive embedded app views.
  - System monitor renders a live monitor UI.
  - QR renders an embedded QR UI.
  - PDF renders an interactive PDF viewer.
  - Restored app views can reconnect after gateway/container restart.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=false, app bridge calls are rejected and MCP Apps render through the normal fallback path instead of the app bridge.

### Actual

  - Verified locally with system-monitor, QR, and PDF MCP Apps examples.
  - Verified upstream image comparison: upstream can call the MCP servers but does not render the MCP Apps UI resources.
  - Verified restart/reload behavior for the PDF app view.

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/aaa93975-4406-493e-a060-4ac64763704b

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
    - No migration is required for existing installs. MCP Apps support is default-off.
    - To enable it, set:
      ```bash
      openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json
      ```
    - Configure MCP servers that expose MCP Apps / `mcp-ui` resources.
    - Restart OpenClaw so the updated config is loaded.

## Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: Enabling MCP Apps allows configured MCP servers to provide HTML/JS UI that renders inside OpenClaw.
    - Mitigation: Feature is opt-in via `mcp.apps.enabled`; app UI renders in a sandboxed iframe; docs and security audit call out the trust boundary.
  - Risk: App views can call app-visible MCP tools/resources through the bridge, increasing the practical surface area of a trusted MCP server.
    - Mitigation: Bridge is scoped to MCP app methods only; model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls; app-only tools are hidden from the model.
  - Risk: Restored chat views may need to reconnect to MCP runtimes after a container restart.
    - Mitigation: Gateway proxy lazily recreates the session MCP runtime from config when an app bridge call arrives and no runtime is currently active.
  - Risk: Servers without MCP Apps support, or installs with the feature disabled, should not regress.
    - Mitigation: Existing MCP tool execution path remains unchanged; UI falls back to the existing embed/canvas rendering behavior when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.
