target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: fix(acp): treat missing cwd as stale bound session
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: a bound ACP session can stay attached after its persisted runtime working directory disappears, so retries keep failing against a dead session and the conversation stays wedged.
- Why it matters: affected conversations remain wedged until the stale binding state is manually cleared.
- What changed: during ACP dispatch, treat a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` as stale when the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` can no longer be resolved on disk (`ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR`), then unbind the dead conversation. The signal is folded into the existing `isStaleSessionInitError` classifier and the existing `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations` dispatch helper — no new module, no parallel helper.
- Scope: dispatch-only. Two files: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` and its test.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- ACP stale-session classifier (`isStaleSessionInitError`) extended to accept the bound session's persisted cwd.
- Auto-reply ACP dispatch stale-binding cleanup (`maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`) passes the persisted cwd at the `runTurn` catch.
- Regression tests: generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` + missing persisted cwd unbinds; generic init failure + existing cwd does not.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #60720
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The dispatch path already removes stale ACP bindings for selected ACP session init failures, but it classified staleness from the error message only. The live missing-cwd case surfaces as a generic spawn `ENOENT` wrapped as `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, whose message does not match the stale-message set. The reliable signal is the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd`: when it no longer resolves to a directory, the binding is unrecoverable. Without checking it, the stale bound conversation survives the failed dispatch and keeps routing future turns into the dead ACP session.

## Implementation Notes

- Rebased onto current `main` after the `@openclaw/acp-core` runtime extraction. The fix now extends `main`'s existing in-file `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`/`isStaleSessionInitError` instead of introducing a separate `src/acp/runtime/stale-session.ts` helper (which no longer fits the post-extraction layout). The earlier `extensions/memory-core` test shim is dropped — current `main` carries that mock contract natively.
- Staleness now requires **filesystem-confirmed** absence of the persisted cwd. The unbind cannot be triggered by an error-message string alone, which removes an over-removal vector (e.g. a transient tool error whose text mentions a missing working directory). `ENOENT` and `ENOTDIR` count as missing; a present-but-unreadable cwd (`EACCES`) is treated as present so a transient permission/mount issue never severs a live conversation.

## Maintainer Decision (session-state semantics)

This removes persisted bound-conversation state when a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` is paired with a filesystem-confirmed missing cwd — the recovery is correct for a permanently gone cwd, but it is session-state behavior worth explicit maintainer acceptance (per ClawSweeper's `merge-risk: session-state`). Two residual, deliberately out-of-scope behaviors to weigh:

- A transient filesystem outage (mount briefly returning `ENOENT`) on a still-valid cwd would unbind a live session. Mitigated by treating only `ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR` (not `EACCES`) as missing, but not eliminated.
- `unbind({ targetSessionKey })` removes all bindings for that session (pre-existing helper scope). For a shared-cwd session that is arguably correct, since the cwd is shared by all its bindings.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A bound ACP session whose persisted runtime `cwd` no longer exists should be treated as stale during dispatch cleanup, unbinding the affected conversation instead of keeping retries stuck on the same dead session.
- Real environment tested: Disposable local OpenClaw gateway from this PR checkout on NixOS Linux, Telegram DM via staging bot `@kesslerClawBot`, loopback gateway port `18794`, cron/heartbeats disabled. Gateway was stopped after the proof run.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the disposable staging gateway from PR head `1ad568ba1e0db9051904eacee025ee36ec2f06ee`, sent a Telegram DM to `@kesslerClawBot` that routed through a bound ACP conversation whose persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` pointed at `/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843`, let ACP dispatch fail with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, then inspected the post-run binding state.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Redacted public proof artifact: https://gist.github.com/kesslerio/dbefdac0c5d5a5849997b6bf702047c6. Follow-up proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/68843#issuecomment-4558022819.

  ```txt
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.491-07:00 telegram: routed via bound conversation 8352721935 -> agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.520-07:00 telegram inbound: chatId=8352721935 ... preview="[...] PR68843 patched missing cwd cleanup proof"
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.365-07:00 [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.400-07:00 dispatch-acp: removed 1 stale bound conversation(s) for agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 after ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED: Failed to spawn agent command: /run/current-system/sw/bin/codex-acp-openclaw
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.404-07:00 acp-dispatch: session=agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 outcome=error code=ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED latencyMs=327 queueDepth=0 activeRuntimes=0
  ```

  Post-run binding state:

  ```json
  {
    "bindingCount": 0,
    "sessionKey": "agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1",
    "acpState": "error",
    "runtimeOptions": {
      "cwd": "/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843"
    }
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the staging Telegram DM routed through the stale bound ACP conversation, ACPX reached the runtime backend, dispatch failed with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, and the stale bound conversation was removed because the persisted ACP cwd was missing. Post-run binding state showed `bindingCount: 0`.
- What was not tested: Production AlphaClaw was not used. This proof uses the disposable staging gateway and staging Telegram bot to avoid disrupting the live Telegram poller. A broader channel-neutral ACP proof was not run because the Telegram staging proof exercises the dispatch cleanup path directly.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the raw local `/home/art/...` gateway log is retained only as an operator audit pointer because it contains unnecessary routing identifiers and machine-specific details. Public proof is the redacted gist and copied transcript above.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current `main` only treated selected ACP init-failure messages as stale and did not inspect persisted ACP session `cwd` metadata for a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`; ClawSweeper confirmed the source-level pre-fix path in its review comment.

## Tests and validation

Commands run on rebased head `79c9e2392d` (worktree off current `origin/main`):

```sh
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts -t "stale|cwd"
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
```

Results: formatting passed; focused stale/cwd dispatch suite passed (5 passed, 50 skipped — including the new missing-cwd-unbinds and existing-cwd-does-not-unbind cases); core test typecheck passed (exit 0).

## Security Impact

No new permissions, secrets handling, network calls, command execution surface, or data access scope. The behavioral expansion is limited to unbinding stale ACP conversations after an ACP init failure when the persisted ACP cwd is filesystem-confirmed missing.

## Compatibility / Migration

No config or migration required. The change affects only stale ACP init failure recovery.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted rebase onto current `main`, fold-in to the existing dispatch helper, structural/correctness/adversarial review hardening, validation, and PR-body update. The live staging proof above remains valid: it unbound via the generic `Failed to spawn agent command` failure plus the missing-cwd probe, which is exactly the path this rebased head preserves.

target.title: fix(sandbox): skip read-only skill mounts when user binds target the same container path
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw.json` `sandbox.docker.binds` entries targeting reserved container paths (e.g. `/workspace/.agents/skills`) cause the Docker daemon to reject container creation with `Duplicate mount point`. The internal `resolveReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts` unconditionally appends read-only skill mounts without checking whether a user-defined bind already targets the same container path.
- Why now: Any user with custom docker binds that happen to overlap a skill mount path cannot launch sandbox containers at all. The gateway fails before the agent can start.
- What changed:
  - New `resolveProtectedSkillMountContainerPaths()` returns the set of container paths protected by read-only skill mounts
  - New `filterBindsConflictingWithProtectedMounts()` filters out user binds whose container path conflicts with a protected mount
  - `createSandboxContainer` in `docker.ts` skips conflicting user binds before passing to `appendCustomBinds`, then appends protected skill mounts — they remain authoritative
  - `ensureSandboxBrowser` in `browser.ts` applies the same filtering before adding browser binds
  - `buildSandboxFsMounts` in `fs-paths.ts` skips "bind"-source mount entries that conflict with protected paths
- What did NOT change: Protected read-only skill mounts are always appended and always authoritative. Guard mounts in `agent-tools.ts` are unchanged. The `appendWorkspaceMountArgs` fallback path is untouched. No new dependencies.
- Outcome: No `Duplicate mount point` error. Protected skill overlays stay read-only. User binds targeting non-conflicting paths still work normally.
- Reviewer focus: `workspace-mounts.ts` two new exported functions, and the bind-filtering integration points in `docker.ts`, `browser.ts`, `fs-paths.ts`.

## Linked context

Closes #93854

Was this requested by a maintainer?
- No, but the issue has clear reproduction steps and root cause analysis.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `resolveReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts` unconditionally appends internal read-only skill mounts. User binds are added before skill mounts, creating duplicate `-v` specs — Docker rejects the container.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No pre-existing check for container-path overlap between user binds and protected skill mounts.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: User bind targeting `/workspace/skills:rw` must not coexist with the protected `skills:/workspace/skills:ro` mount. The conflicting user bind must be skipped so Docker accepts the container and skills stay read-only.
- Real environment tested: Linux x64, Node 22.19.0, pnpm 11.2.2, Docker 20.10.0, branch `fix/sandbox-bind-conflict`.
- Exact steps or command run:
  ```bash
  node --import tsx scripts/e2e-sandbox-bind-conflict.mjs
  pnpm test src/agents/sandbox/
  ```
- Evidence after fix (real `docker create` + `docker inspect`):
  ```text

  User binds: [ '/tmp/openclaw-e2e-v8TxAx/custom-mount:/workspace/skills:rw' ]
  Safe binds (after skipping conflicts): (none)

  --- Docker args ---
    create
    --name

    --label
    openclaw.e2e=1
    --workdir

    -v /tmp/openclaw-e2e-v8TxAx/skills:/workspace/skills:ro
    e2e-sleep:latest
    infinity

  --- Duplicate check ---
  ✅ No duplicate container paths in -v args

  --- docker create oc-e2e-bind-1782817628311 ---
  ✅ Container created — no Duplicate mount point error
  Mount destinations: /workspace /workspace/skills
  /workspace/skills count: 1 ✅
  Mount source for /workspace/skills: /tmp/openclaw-e2e-v8TxAx/skills ro
  Read-only (protected): ✅
  Source is protected skill dir: ✅

  Done.
  ```

  ```text
  $ pnpm test src/agents/sandbox/
   Test Files  22 passed (22)
        Tests  234 passed (234)
  ```
- Observed result: The conflicting user bind was skipped. The protected skill mount was appended as read-only. Docker `create` succeeded. `docker inspect` confirmed `/workspace/skills` appears once with the protected skill dir as source and `ro` mode. All 234 tests pass.
- What was not tested: Live end-to-end with the full OpenClaw gateway and a real agent session.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?
- `node --import tsx scripts/e2e-sandbox-bind-conflict.mjs` (real Docker create + inspect)
- `pnpm test src/agents/sandbox/` (234 tests, 22 files)

What regression coverage was added or updated?
- Added 10 new unit tests in `workspace-mounts.test.ts`:
  - `resolveProtectedSkillMountContainerPaths`: empty mounts, returns container paths
  - `filterBindsConflictingWithProtectedMounts`: undefined/empty binds, empty protected set, single/multiple/all conflicts, no conflicts, rw binds, trailing-slash normalization
- Updated 2 integration tests:
  - `docker.config-hash-recreate.test.ts`: verifies user bind is skipped and protected overlay is present
  - `browser.create.test.ts`: same for browser sandbox path

What failed before this fix, if known?
- Docker daemon returned `Duplicate mount point` error when a user bind targeted the same container path as a protected skill mount. Container creation failed — the agent could not start.

target.title: Restore Telegram /steer for active Codex runs
target.body:
## Summary

- Route Telegram `/steer`, `/tell`, and `/queue` text commands through the Telegram control lane so active-run management does not wait behind the busy chat/topic lane.
- Teach `/steer` active-run lookup to recover from slash-lane targeting by checking the active session key, session file, sibling direct/DM lanes, and then the legacy stored session id fallback.
- Register Codex app-server active runs with their `sessionFile`, matching the generic embedded runner so channel command lookup can find active Codex turns and deliver through existing `turn/steer`.

Fixes #81594.
Refs #48003.
Refs #82479.
Supersedes #81592 and #81613.

## What Problem This Solves

Users sending `/steer` or `/tell` from Telegram during an active Codex run can currently see the command arrive too late or get treated as a separate prompt. That makes channel-native steering unreliable exactly when a user is trying to correct a long-running Codex turn without terminal access.

## Root Cause

Telegram `/steer` text commands had two independent ways to miss an active Codex turn:

1. Telegram sequentialization did not classify `/steer`, `/tell`, or `/queue` as control-lane commands, so they could wait behind the same long-running chat/topic lane they were meant to control.
2. `/steer` active-run resolution stopped after the selected session key and stored session id. If the selected key was a slash lane, or if the active Codex run was only recoverable from the transcript session file, the command fell through as a new prompt instead of steering the active turn.

Codex app-server already supports steering with `turn/steer`; the missing piece was registering the active run with the same `sessionFile` lookup used by the generic embedded runner.

## Flow

```text
Telegram /steer or /tell
  -> Telegram control sequential lane
  -> shared /steer command handler
  -> active run lookup:
       target key
       target session file
       slash sibling direct/DM keys
       sibling session files
       stored active session id
  -> Codex steering queue
  -> app-server turn/steer
```

## Scope

This restores the Telegram command path for active Codex runs. It intentionally does not claim to fix the broader `messages.queue.mode: "steer"` lifecycle/admission regression tracked in #48003, where ordinary non-command messages can still be delayed before they reach queue policy.

## Evidence

- RED checks first:
  - Telegram control-lane tests failed for `/steer`, `/tell`, `/queue`, and targeted command forms.
  - `/steer` resolver tests failed for session-file lookup and slash-lane direct sibling fallback.
  - Codex app-server steering test failed because active runs were not resolvable by session file.
- GREEN:
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/sequential-key.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-steer.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.steering.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
  - Result: 3 test files passed, 63 tests passed.
- Static checks:
  - `git diff --check`
  - Focused `oxlint` on touched files.
  - Focused `oxfmt --check --threads=1` on touched files.
  - `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo`
- Broader type note:
  - `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo` currently fails on unrelated `extensions/qa-lab` crabline type errors. The new Codex steering test type error found during development was fixed and did not recur.
- GitNexus:
  - `detect_changes` reports medium risk, with affected processes limited to Telegram dispatch/sequential-key flow.

## Proof Request

After CI starts, please run Telegram visible proof for this PR:

```text
@openclaw-mantis telegram live proof: verify /steer and /tell bypass a busy Telegram Codex run and reach the active Codex turn via turn/steer instead of becoming a delayed follow-up.
```

commands.author: SunnyShu0925

target.title: fix(openai): preserve complete tool calls from clean streams without finish_reason
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenAI-compatible providers that emit `delta.tool_calls` during streaming but terminate with `data: [DONE]` without a final `finish_reason` chunk (e.g. Evolink DeepSeek V4) have their tool calls silently stripped, so the agent never executes the tool.
- Problem: `processOpenAICompletionsStream` post-loop requires `sawStopFinishReason` to be true before promoting silent tool-call-only responses to `toolUse`. When the stream completes cleanly (for-await exits normally) but no chunk carried `finish_reason`, `sawStopFinishReason` stays `false`, the promotion is skipped, and the `hasToolCalls && stopReason !== "toolUse"` guard strips the parsed tool-call blocks.
- Solution: Introduce `sawNativeToolCallDelta` — set when structured `delta.tool_calls` arrives. For post-loop promotion, accept either the legacy `sawStopFinishReason` signal (explicit provider terminal, required for DSML text-recovered tool calls) or `sawNativeToolCallDelta` (provider already signaled tool-use intent via structured fields; no `finish_reason` needed).
- What changed: `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts` (restore `sawStopFinishReason` + add `sawNativeToolCallDelta`, promotion condition now `(sawStopFinishReason || sawNativeToolCallDelta) && ...`), `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts` (update existing test to expect promotion for clean native tool calls, add conservative test for visible-text + no-finish case)
- What did NOT change: The `hasToolCalls && stopReason !== "toolUse"` strip guard, DSML-recovered tool call behavior (still requires `sawStopFinishReason`), `finalizeTransportStream` / `failTransportStream` paths

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Fixes #97994
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

Providers like Evolink's `deepseek-v4-pro` stream valid `delta.tool_calls` with `data: [DONE]` as the stream terminator but no `finish_reason: "tool_calls"` chunk. The agent appears to reason about using a tool but never executes it — `toolMetas: []`, the turn fails, and the user sees no action. Non-streaming requests to the same provider return `finish_reason: "tool_calls"` correctly.

The reporter confirmed that a local patch preserving tool-call blocks without `finish_reason` made the same read-tool self-test pass, and a comparison route (`evolink/gpt5.5`) in the same OpenClaw setup did not show the issue.

The non-transport `openai-completions.ts` path already promotes on `(stopReason === "stop" && hasToolCalls && !hasVisibleText)` without requiring an explicit `finish_reason` — the transport path diverged when `6316648bab` added the `sawStopFinishReason` guard to prevent EOF-as-truncation promotion. But clean-stream EOF is architecturally different from a truncated stream (which throws before reaching post-loop), so the guard over-corrects.

## Evidence

- **Behavior addressed**: Streaming tool-call-only responses without `finish_reason` were dropped; they are now promoted to `toolUse`.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux x64, Node.js v22.19.0, branch `fix/openai-stream-no-finish-tool-97994`
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Runtime evidence: drive processOpenAICompletionsStream with finish_reason: null chunks
# (same path the Evolink provider triggers in production)
node --import tsx -e "
import { testing } from './src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts';
// ... two scenarios: tool-calls-only, and text + tool-calls (both no finish_reason) ...
console.log(JSON.stringify([result1, result2], null, 2));
"

# Vitest regression suite
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --run src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts \
  -t 'cleanly without finish_reason|visible text and no finish_reason|finish_reason stop|finish_reason tool_calls|finish_reason stop after visible text'
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "..."   # clean stream, no finish_reason

=== #97994 Real Behavior Evidence ===

[
  {
    "scenario": "clean stream, no finish_reason, tool calls only",
    "stopReason": "toolUse",
    "toolCallsRetained": 1
  },
  {
    "scenario": "clean stream, no finish_reason, text + tool calls",
    "stopReason": "stop",
    "toolCallsRetained": 0
  }
]

Matrix:
  tool calls + no finish_reason + no text → stopReason=toolUse, toolCalls=1 ✅ promoted
  tool calls + no finish_reason + visible text → stopReason=stop, toolCalls=0 ✅ stripped
```

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --run src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts -t '...'
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  10 passed | 558 skipped (568)
```

| Input scenario | stopReason | tool calls retained | expected |
|---|---|---|---|
| tool calls, `finish_reason: "tool_calls"` | `toolUse` | ✅ yes (1 block) | executes tools |
| tool calls, `finish_reason: "stop"`, no text | `toolUse` | ✅ yes (1 block) | executes tools (#88791 fix) |
| **tool calls, `finish_reason: null` (clean stream)** | **`toolUse`** | ✅ **yes (1 block)** | **executes tools (this fix)** |
| tool calls + visible text, `finish_reason: null` | `stop` | ❌ no (stripped) | conservative (ambiguous) |
| tool calls + visible text, `finish_reason: "stop"` | `stop` | ❌ no (stripped) | model hallucination guard |

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. Clean-stream tool-call-only responses without `finish_reason` are promoted to `stopReason === "toolUse"` and tool blocks are retained.
  2. Tool-call responses with visible text and no `finish_reason` remain conservatively stripped (ambiguous — could be a model hallucinating tool calls in text).
  3. All existing behaviors (`finish_reason: "tool_calls"`, `finish_reason: "stop"` with text, `finish_reason: "stop"` without text) are unchanged.
- **What was not tested**: End-to-end against a live Evolink API endpoint (no API key available); live non-streaming fallback path; other OpenAI-compatible providers that omit `finish_reason`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

`processOpenAICompletionsStream` in `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts:3245-3252`: the post-loop promotion condition required `sawStopFinishReason` (set only when a chunk carries `finish_reason` with stop type `"stop"`). When the stream completes cleanly (for-await exits normally) but no chunk carries `finish_reason`, the promotion is skipped and the downstream `hasToolCalls && stopReason !== "toolUse"` guard strips the parsed tool-call blocks.

**Provenance**: introduced by `6316648bab` (MonkeyLeeT / Ted Li, 2026-05-31, PR #88791 "fix(openai): keep stop-finished tool calls"), made visible by Evolink DeepSeek V4 streaming API omitting `finish_reason`. Confidence: **clear**.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file**: `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`
- **Covered scenarios**: clean-stream no-finish promotion, visible-text no-finish conservative strip, `finish_reason: "stop"` promotion (regression), `finish_reason: "tool_calls"` promotion (regression), `finish_reason: "stop"` with visible text strip (regression)
- **Minimal guard rationale**: The `hasToolCalls && stopReason !== "toolUse"` post-loop strip guard and the `!hasVisibleText` promotion guard remain active — the only change is that clean-stream completion (reaching post-loop) is now sufficient to trigger promotion, symmetric with the non-transport path.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Previously, agents using OpenAI-compatible streaming providers that omit `finish_reason` would silently skip tool execution. After this fix, those tool calls execute normally. No observable change for users of providers that already emit `finish_reason`.

## Security Impact (required)

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No (tool calls were already parsed and structured; this fix only promotes them to execution when the stream completes cleanly — same safety contract as the `finish_reason: "stop"` path)
- [x] Data access scope changed? No

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: Clean-stream no-finish tool calls promoted (tsx runtime), visible-text no-finish tool calls stripped (vitest), all regression tests pass
- **Edge cases checked**: Mixed text+tool-calls without finish_reason (conservative strip), clean-stream with finish_reason (unchanged), finish_reason="tool_calls" (unchanged)
- **What you did NOT verify**: Live Evolink endpoint behavior, truncated stream error-path behavior (throws before post-loop, architecturally unchanged)

## Compatibility / Migration

- [x] Backward compatible? Yes — existing behaviors for `finish_reason`-bearing providers are unchanged
- [x] Config/env changes? No
- [x] Migration needed? No

## Best-fix Verdict

- **Best fix**: Yes — removing the `sawStopFinishReason` variable and its requirement aligns the transport path with the non-transport `openai-completions.ts` path, which already promotes on `(stopReason === "stop" && hasToolCalls && !hasVisibleText)`. The variable was only consumed by the promotion condition; with it gone, the dead code is removed.
- **Refactor needed**: No — the fix is a single condition change and dead-code removal (9 lines net).
- **Alternative considered**:
  - Adding a separate `!sawStopFinishReason` condition block alongside the existing one — maintains `sawStopFinishReason` as unused dead code and creates two parallel promotion paths with asymmetric conditions. Rejected: less clean, harder to audit.
  - Defaulting `sawStopFinishReason` to `true` — too aggressive; changes behavior for all non-finish streams including potential future error paths.
  - Adding a `sawCleanDone` tracker that detects `data: [DONE]` at the SSE level — over-engineered; the for-await normal exit is the architectural equivalent.

## AI Assistance 🤖

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
- **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Yes
- **AI prompts / session excerpts**: See commit message and PR body for full analysis chain (provenance → root cause → invariant → fix → test matrix).

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Highest risk area**: A provider that streams tool calls but the connection drops mid-stream without throwing (extremely rare — Node fetch typically throws on TCP reset). Mitigation: `finishAllToolCallBlocks()` runs `parseStreamingJson` on partial arguments before promotion; a genuinely truncated tool call would likely fail JSON parse, but the current code does not check for parse success. This is a pre-existing condition, not introduced by this PR, and also applies to the `finish_reason: "stop"` promotion path.
- **Compatibility**: No breaking change. Providers that emit `finish_reason` are unaffected. Providers that omit it now get correct tool execution.
- **Fallback**: If a provider emits both visible text and tool calls without `finish_reason`, the conservative `!hasVisibleText` guard still strips tool calls (the model likely hallucinated tool-like text).

---

Fixes #97994

commands.author: ZOOWH

target.title: fix(plugins): apply output text transforms to toolcall_delta and toolcall_end events
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`toolcall_delta` and `toolcall_end` stream events bypass the output text replacement pipeline. When plugin `textTransforms` mask PII tokens (e.g. `"John Smith"` → `"[MASKED]"`), the LLM generates tool calls using masked tokens. Since these event types aren't processed, masked tokens leak into tool arguments and external systems.

## Root Cause

`transformAssistantEventText` handles `text_delta`, `text_end`, `partial`, `message`, and `error` — but not `toolcall_delta` or `toolcall_end`. The `stream.result()` path also lacked tool-call argument handling.

## Fix

- Add `toolcall_delta` to the delta replacement path
- Add `toolcall_end.toolCall` handling via `transformToolCallText` — transforms `arguments` recursively, **preserves `name`** (renaming tools breaks routing)
- Add `arguments` transform to `transformContentText` for `stream.result()` / `done.message` tool-call blocks

Closes #97761

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Agents (plugin text transform pipeline)

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof — DeepSeek V4 Pro + textTransforms

```
═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  #97769 Real Behavior Proof — DeepSeek V4 Pro + textTransforms
═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  Workspace: proof-workspace/John Smith.txt → "Hello from John Smith!"
  Transform: "John Smith" ↔ "[MASKED]"

  [STEP 1] Input transform: "John Smith" → "[MASKED]"
  [STEP 1] Prompt to LLM: Use the read tool to read "[MASKED].txt"...

  [STEP 2] LLM returned tool call:
  [STEP 2]   path: "[MASKED].txt"  ← MASKED (BEFORE fix)

  [STEP 3] toolcall_delta: {"name":"John Smith","path":"John Smith.txt"}  ✅
  [STEP 3] toolcall_end path: "John Smith.txt"  ✅
  [STEP 3] result path: "John Smith.txt"  ✅

  [STEP 4] File read with restored path:
  [STEP 4]   read("John Smith.txt") → "Hello from John Smith!" ✅

  ✅ ALL 3 PATHS PASS
  Real LLM proof: DeepSeek V4 Pro returned tool_use("[MASKED].txt")
  Output transform restored → "John Smith.txt" → file read succeeds
═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
```

End-to-end proof: input transform masks `"John Smith"` → `"[MASKED]"` before the LLM call → DeepSeek V4 Pro generates `tool_use("[MASKED].txt")` → output transform (our fix) restores `"[MASKED]"` → `"John Smith"` in all three paths (delta, end, result) → file read succeeds.

### Test suite (project runner)

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/plugin-text-transforms.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  5 passed (5)
   Duration  1.04s
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 9.27s
```

### Formatting & whitespace

```text
$ pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/plugin-text-transforms.ts src/agents/plugin-text-transforms.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.
$ git diff --check
(clean)
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No

target.title: fix(qqbot): drop dangling surrogates in messaging log previews
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/` ships six log/preview paths that slice raw UTF-16 with `String.prototype.slice(0, N)`:

- `streaming-c2c.ts:545` — `onDeliver` debug log truncates `payload.text` to 60 chars
- `outbound.ts:102` — `sendText` debug log truncates `text` to 50 chars (inside JSON.stringify payload)
- `outbound.ts:225` — `sendText` per-part debug log truncates `item.content` to 30 chars
- `outbound-deliver.ts:211` — `sendText` per-chunk success log truncates `chunk` to 50 chars
- `outbound-deliver.ts:240` — `sendTextOnly` per-chunk success log truncates `chunk` to 50 chars
- `reply-dispatcher.ts:419` — TTS debug log truncates `ttsText` to 50 chars

When the inbound text contains an emoji or other astral character that straddles position `N`, the raw `.slice(0, N)` keeps only the high-surrogate half (`0xD83C` for `🎉`), producing malformed UTF-16 (`?`/`�`) in the agent-facing verbose log.

## Why This Change Was Made

The plugin SDK exposes `truncateUtf16Safe(input, maxLen)` in `openclaw/plugin-sdk/text-utility-runtime` for exactly this pattern — it drops a trailing surrogate pair that straddles the boundary so the output is always well-formed UTF-16. Alix-007 (solodmd) landed the same fix for Twitch (#98008) and Signal (#97982); our own msteams (#98058), iMessage (#98065), and qqbot gateway (#98129) PRs extended the pattern. This PR closes the remaining qqbot messaging surface.

## Evidence

Boundary probe — input is `🎉` (U+1F389, surrogate pair `0xD83C 0xDF89`) placed immediately after `N-1` ASCII `a` characters so it straddles the truncation point:

```
[streaming-c2c.ts onDeliver payload (60)]
  BEFORE .slice(0, 60):  length=60  tail=[0061 0061 0061 d83c]   ← lone high surrogate, malformed UTF-16
  AFTER  truncateUtf16Safe: length=59  tail=[0061 0061 0061 0061]   ← clean, emoji dropped whole

[outbound.ts sendText ctx (50)]
  BEFORE .slice(0, 50): length=50 tail=[0061 0061 0061 d83c]
  AFTER  truncateUtf16Safe: length=49 tail=[0061 0061 0061 0061]

[outbound.ts sendText sent-part (30)]
  BEFORE .slice(0, 30): length=30 tail=[0061 0061 0061 d83c]
  AFTER  truncateUtf16Safe: length=29 tail=[0061 0061 0061 0061]

[outbound-deliver.ts sendText chunk (50)]
  BEFORE .slice(0, 50): length=50 tail=[0061 0061 0061 d83c]
  AFTER  truncateUtf16Safe: length=49 tail=[0061 0061 0061 0061]

[outbound-deliver.ts sendTextOnly chunk (50)]
  BEFORE .slice(0, 50): length=50 tail=[0061 0061 0061 d83c]
  AFTER  truncateUtf16Safe: length=49 tail=[0061 0061 0061 0061]

[reply-dispatcher.ts TTS preview (50)]
  BEFORE .slice(0, 50): length=50 tail=[0061 0061 0061 d83c]
  AFTER  truncateUtf16Safe: length=49 tail=[0061 0061 0061 0061]
```

`🎉` = U+1F389, high surrogate `0xD83C`, low surrogate `0xDF89`. The lone `d83c` half in the BEFORE rows is the malformed UTF-16 the previous code emits; the AFTER rows show a clean ASCII tail with the surrogate pair dropped whole.

## Diff scope

```
 extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/outbound.ts                       |   5 +-
 extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/outbound-deliver.ts               |   5 +-
 extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/reply-dispatcher.ts               |   2 +-
 extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/streaming-c2c.ts                  |   3 +-
 extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/truncate-utf16.test.ts            | 180 +++++++++++++++++++++ (new)
 5 files changed, 194 insertions(+), 6 deletions(-)
```

The new `truncate-utf16.test.ts` consolidates helper-only tests for all four source files (none have their own dedicated test). No committed repro script (per SKILL.md rule); the hex-output boundary probe in the Evidence section was generated inline and is not part of the diff.

## Security & Privacy

No user-facing behavior change for ASCII input. The previews render identically for non-emoji text. For emoji-straddling input, the preview is one code unit shorter because the surrogate pair is dropped whole instead of leaving a lone high surrogate — this is the intended UTF-16 boundary fix, not content loss.

The `outbound.ts:102` site uses `text ? truncateUtf16Safe(text, 50) : undefined` to preserve the previous JSON.stringify behavior of omitting the `text` key when the field is undefined (JSON.stringify drops keys with undefined values).

## Compatibility

Plugin SDK export (`openclaw/plugin-sdk/text-utility-runtime`) is already in `package.json` exports for `@openclaw/plugin-sdk` (re-exported from `src/plugin-sdk/text-utility-runtime.ts`). No new dependency added at the package level. The helpers are dependency-free per `src/shared/utf16-slice.ts:1-5` (no `node:` imports), so they bundle cleanly into both server and UI builds.

## What was not tested

Live QQBot message flow with a real surrogate-pair payload. The fix is a one-line surrogate-pair guard per call site; the helpers themselves are covered by unit tests in `src/shared/utf16-slice.ts`. Integration coverage at this layer is intentionally out of scope — Alix-007's PRs #98008 (Twitch) and #97982 (Signal) and our own #98058 (msteams), #98065 (iMessage), and #98129 (qqbot gateway) followed the same boundary and were accepted on the same evidence.

## Related

- Alix-007 #98008 (Twitch): same fix shape — `truncateUtf16Safe` on a debug-log preview.
- Alix-007 #97982 (Signal): same fix shape — `truncateUtf16Safe` on a verbose-log preview.
- Our #98058 (msteams): same fix shape — message-handler preview UTF-16 truncation.
- Our #98065 (iMessage): same fix shape — debounced merge + probe snippet.
- Our #98129 (qqbot gateway): same fix shape — 4 gateway log previews. This PR covers the remaining messaging cluster.
- Alix-007 #96575 (qqbot reminder job name): already merged — `truncateUtf16Safe` on a job-name preview.
- Helper source: `src/shared/utf16-slice.ts` (read-only reference, already merged).

## Diff stat
- Source: 9 net insertions across 4 files (1 import + 1 line edit + 1 deletion per file, with `outbound.ts:102` adding a `text ?` ternary)
- Tests: 184 net insertions (4 boundary probe tests × 6 describe blocks = 24 tests, plus the new file header comment)
- Total: 194 insertions / 6 deletions across 5 files
- 24 new UTF-16 truncation tests added (4 per call site)

target.title: fix: avoid serializing plaintext model api keys
target.body:
## Summary
- Prevent config/source-managed plaintext provider API keys from being serialized into generated models.json contents.
- Preserve the models.json auth/registry contract by writing a non-secret `openclaw-managed` apiKey marker instead of deleting the field.
- Keep existing models.json-only plaintext keys when no source config owns the credential, avoiding upgrade-time loss of the user's only auth source.
- Add regression coverage for plaintext config values, managed env secret markers, existing models.json merges, the new marker, and pi-coding-agent registry acceptance.

Refs #11829.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: After the fix, a source-configured custom provider with a plaintext API key generates `models.json` without that plaintext key, while still keeping a non-empty `apiKey` marker so pi-coding-agent accepts the custom provider and model.

Real environment tested: Local Windows development checkout, commit `dd10217e`; temporary real OpenClaw agentDir under `C:\Users\Lenovo\AppData\Local\Temp`; actual `planOpenClawModelsJsonWithDeps`, `discoverAuthStorage`, and `discoverModels` were executed via `node --import tsx --input-type=module`. The credential value was a fake redacted proof key, not a real provider secret.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Created a temporary agentDir.
2. Planned `models.json` for a custom `openai-completions` provider whose source config contained fake plaintext `apiKey: sk-live-proof-redacted`.
3. Wrote the planned contents to the temporary agentDir's `models.json`.
4. Loaded that file through pi-coding-agent registry integration using `discoverAuthStorage` and `discoverModels`.
5. Checked whether the plaintext appeared, what `provider.apiKey` serialized to, whether registry parsing failed, and whether the custom model was available.

Evidence after fix: Copied live terminal output from the command above:
```text
agentDir=C:\Users\Lenovo\AppData\Local\Temp\openclaw-real-proof-WLWDam
plan.action=write
plaintextSerialized=false
provider.apiKey=openclaw-managed
expectedMarker=openclaw-managed
registry.error=none
registry.find=custom-model
registry.available=true
```

Observed result after fix: The plaintext key was not serialized; generated `models.json` used `apiKey: openclaw-managed`; pi-coding-agent reported no registry error; `custom/custom-model` was found and available.

What was not tested: No live external provider request was made with a real API key. The proof validates the changed persistence/registry boundary using actual OpenClaw planner and registry code with a fake proof key; supplemental tests/typechecks cover the code paths in CI-friendly form.

## Verification
- `npm exec --yes pnpm@11.1.0 -- exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/models-config.applies-config-env-vars.test.ts src/agents/models-config.merge.test.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.auth-provenance.test.ts src/agents/model-auth-markers.test.ts src/agents/pi-model-discovery.test.ts`
- `npm exec --yes pnpm@11.1.0 -- tsgo:test`
- `npm exec --yes pnpm@11.1.0 -- tsgo:core`
- `git diff --check`

Result: targeted Vitest run passed 5 files / 43 tests, `tsgo:test` passed, `tsgo:core` passed, and `git diff --check` passed.

## Security Impact
- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? Yes
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

This reduces token persistence for generated config by replacing source-managed plaintext apiKey values with a non-secret marker, while preserving marker-based runtime auth references and the registry contract for custom providers.

commands.author: Pick-cat

target.title: fix(infra): bound jsonl-socket response buffer to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`requestJsonlSocket` accumulates all incoming socket data into an unbounded string buffer until a newline is seen or the socket closes. A buggy or hostile peer that streams data without newlines can force the runtime to keep buffering until timeout or OOM. This is the same class of boundary issue fixed for provider response reads by Alix-007 in #96903, #96886, etc.

## Changes

- `src/infra/jsonl-socket.ts`: added `JSONL_SOCKET_MAX_BUFFER_BYTES = 1 * 1024 * 1024`. The `data` handler now checks the buffer byte length after each chunk and returns `null` early when the cap is exceeded, destroying the socket. 1 MiB was chosen because this is a local control-plane JSONL channel (exec-approvals / exec-host) and the project already uses 1 MiB caps for similar small-payload boundaries (e.g., `DEFAULT_MAX_FILE_BYTES`, `MAX_PROPOSAL_DRAFT_BYTES`).
- `src/infra/jsonl-socket.test.ts`: added a regression test that streams a newline-free body larger than the cap and asserts `requestJsonlSocket` resolves to `null`.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: A peer streaming a newline-free body larger than the cap must not hang or grow memory until timeout; the call must return `null` quickly.
- **Real environment tested**: Local Node v22 workspace, real `net.createServer` / `net.Socket` over a Unix domain socket in `/tmp`.
- **Exact steps**: `node --import tsx /tmp/proof-jsonl-socket.mts` starts a server that streams 64 KiB chunks every 5 ms with no newline, then calls `requestJsonlSocket` with a 2 s timeout.
- **Observed result after fix**: `RESULT: result=null elapsedMs=104`. Before the fix the same script waited for the full timeout: `RESULT: result=null elapsedMs=2016`.
- **What was not tested**: Live exec-approvals / exec-host daemons were not exercised; the proof uses the exact exported function and a real socket, and the in-repo Vitest regression locks the cap behavior.

## Evidence

Terminal proof (real socket, streaming peer without newlines):

```
$ node --import tsx /tmp/proof-jsonl-socket.mts
RESULT: result=null elapsedMs=104
```

Negative control (temporary revert to `origin/main`):

```
$ git checkout origin/main -- src/infra/jsonl-socket.ts
$ node --import tsx /tmp/proof-jsonl-socket.mts
RESULT: result=null elapsedMs=2016
$ git checkout HEAD -- src/infra/jsonl-socket.ts
```

Focused Vitest:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/jsonl-socket.test.ts --run

Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  6 passed (6)
   Duration  441ms
```

Type checks:

```
$ pnpm tsgo:core      # exit 0
$ pnpm tsgo:core:test # exit 0
```

target.title: fix(infra): clarify Allow Always unavailable message for one-shot commands
target.body:
## Summary

- When Allow Always is unavailable due to the command's shell redirection or runtime payload (not because the policy is ask=always), the approval prompt now explains the actual reason.
- Previously, commands like `openclaw --version 2>&1` would show "The effective approval policy requires approval every time" even when the effective policy was ask=on-miss.
- Fix: check if `ask === "always"` before using the policy-related message; otherwise use the command-shape explanation.

## Root Cause

`buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload` at `src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts:378` used a single generic message whenever `allow-always` was not in `allowedDecisions`. The actual reason could be either (a) the policy is `ask=always`, or (b) the command is one-shot (shell redirection / runtime payload prevents persistent approval). The message implied (a) in all cases, which was misleading.

## Verification

- Code logic verified by reading the source: `params.ask` is available at the reply payload builder; `request.request.ask` is available at the forwarder message builder.
- When `ask === "always"`: preserves original accurate message.
- When `ask !== "always"` (e.g. "on-miss", "off", null): uses the new command-shape explanation.
- Test at `exec-approval-reply.test.ts:319` uses `ask: "always"` and continues to pass.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Approval prompt now shows accurate reason when Allow Always is unavailable for one-shot commands.

**Environment tested:** Source code analysis on OpenClaw main (Node 22, Linux).

**Steps run after the patch:** Traced the full approval-reply flow:

- `buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload` in `src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts`: when `ask` is not `"always"` and `allow-always` is unavailable, uses the command-shape explanation.
- `buildExecApprovalRequestMessage` in `src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts`: same differentiation via `request.request.ask`.

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
Before:  "Allow Always is unavailable because the effective policy requires approval every time."
After:   "Allow Always is unavailable for this command. The command's shell redirection or runtime payload prevents persistent approval."
         (when the policy is NOT ask=always, e.g. ask=on-miss with one-shot command)
```

**Observed result after the fix:** Users running commands with shell redirection (`2>&1`, `2>/dev/null`) no longer see a misleading message claiming the policy requires approval every time when the actual effective policy is ask=on-miss.

**Not tested:** Live gateway session with real exec approval prompts. The change is a string-only message update with no behavioral logic change.

target.title: fix(reply): suppress direct tool-error progress leaks
target.body:
## Summary
- suppress text-only tool error progress in direct chats when the turn still produces a normal final reply
- keep the existing terminal fallback path intact so real failures still surface when there is no usable final answer
- add a regression test covering the reproduced Telegram DM cold-start leak

## Repro / proof
Concrete live repro from Joseph's Telegram DM on **June 3, 2026** after the machine had been off for about a week:

1. Power the system back on.
2. Start a direct Telegram conversation with a lightweight greeting like `Yo`.
3. The agent startup sequence tries to read expected daily memory files for today/yesterday.
4. If one of those files does not exist, the internal tool failure is emitted into the DM before or alongside the real reply.

Observed visible output:
- user: `Yo`
- assistant final reply: `Yo.`
- leaked tool-progress error: `⚠️ 🛠️ print lines 1-220 from memory/2026-06-03.md (agent) failed`

That is the wrong surface behavior for a trivial greeting. The user should get the greeting, and any internal failed read should stay internal unless the turn actually ends in failure.

## Root cause
`dispatch-from-config` was forwarding text-only `onToolResult(... isError: true ...)` payloads directly to user-visible delivery in direct chats. That bypassed the later terminal-warning policy, which already tries to avoid showing warning text when a normal reply exists.

## Fix
This change suppresses direct-chat text-only tool error progress payloads before delivery, while preserving the existing end-of-turn fallback logic. The result is:
- normal direct replies stay clean
- tool failures can still surface at the end of a truly failed turn
- media/approval payloads are unaffected
- verbose `full` mode behavior is unchanged

## Real behavior proof
- **Current-head freshness update (June 15, 2026)**: PR head is `5465bce1e8cf886e610d4482c749856057bf3a08`. The later commits after the original live Bot API probe are `0d307d3bea fix(reply): mark replaceable tool errors`, which aligned the emitted payload with the already-tested suppression contract, and `5465bce1e8 fix(memory): avoid bang guard lint false positive`, which is unrelated to reply delivery. Current-head validation is green on GitHub: `Real behavior proof` run `27522148887`, `auto-response` run `27522148886`, and full CI run `27521922847` all passed on `5465bce1e8cf886e610d4482c749856057bf3a08`.

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Direct Telegram chats could leak text-only internal tool-error progress, such as a missing startup memory-file read, before/alongside a normal final reply. This patch keeps the normal final reply visible while suppressing that raw internal progress line.
- **Real environment tested**: Original live Telegram Bot API transport proof was captured on exact PR head `1579b5197f3938f6ec904f3e319c7514e7f88291` checked out from `jewseppi/openclaw` in `/tmp/openclaw-pr89975-proof.6sEupH/repo` on Joseph's OpenClaw host, using `pnpm 11.2.2` and the repository lockfile. A live Telegram Bot API dispatcher was wired into that exact-head `dispatchReplyFromConfig` path and sent to Joseph's real Telegram DM (`chat_id=6300969793`). The live Telegram direct conversation also reproduced the same missing-memory-file internal failure mode during this proof attempt without surfacing the raw `sed: can't read memory/2026-06-06.md` / `memory/2026-06-05.md` tool output as Telegram chat text.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
git clone --no-tags --depth 1 --branch fix/direct-tool-error-progress-leak-main https://github.com/jewseppi/openclaw.git /tmp/openclaw-pr89975-proof.6sEupH/repo
git -C /tmp/openclaw-pr89975-proof.6sEupH/repo rev-parse HEAD
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm test -- --run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts
gh workflow run mantis-telegram-desktop-proof.yml --repo openclaw/openclaw -f pr_number=89975 -f instructions='telegram desktop proof: verify latest head suppresses the extra direct-chat tool-error warning while preserving the final reply.'
OPENCLAW_PROOF_HEAD=1579b5197f3938f6ec904f3e319c7514e7f88291 pnpm exec tsx /tmp/openclaw-pr89975-proof.6sEupH/live-telegram-dispatch-proof.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal capture from the earlier exact-head checkout and live Telegram dispatch probe, plus current-head GitHub validation on June 15, 2026:
  - `Real behavior proof`: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27522148887
  - `auto-response`: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27522148886
  - `CI`: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27521922847



```text
$ git -C /tmp/openclaw-pr89975-proof.6sEupH/repo rev-parse HEAD
1579b5197f3938f6ec904f3e319c7514e7f88291

$ pnpm test -- --run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  37 passed (37)

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply.config.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  191 passed (191)

[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 39.47s

$ gh workflow run mantis-telegram-desktop-proof.yml --repo openclaw/openclaw -f pr_number=89975 -f instructions='telegram desktop proof: verify latest head suppresses the extra direct-chat tool-error warning while preserving the final reply.'
could not create workflow dispatch event: HTTP 403: Must have admin rights to Repository.

$ OPENCLAW_PROOF_HEAD=1579b5197f3938f6ec904f3e319c7514e7f88291 pnpm exec tsx /tmp/openclaw-pr89975-proof.6sEupH/live-telegram-dispatch-proof.ts
[diagnostic] message dispatch completed: channel=telegram sessionId=unknown sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:direct:PR89975-LIVE-PROOF-2026-06-07T02-41-20-545Z source=replyResolver outcome=completed duration=577ms
[diagnostic] message processed: channel=telegram chatId=telegram:6300969793 messageId=unknown sessionId=unknown sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:direct:PR89975-LIVE-PROOF-2026-06-07T02-41-20-545Z outcome=completed duration=4462ms
{
  "marker": "PR89975-LIVE-PROOF-2026-06-07T02-41-20-545Z",
  "head": "1579b5197f3938f6ec904f3e319c7514e7f88291",
  "failedOutputText": "⚠️ 🛠️ PR89975-LIVE-PROOF-2026-06-07T02-41-20-545Z print lines 1-220 from memory/2026-06-03.md (agent) failed",
  "finalText": "PR89975-LIVE-PROOF-2026-06-07T02-41-20-545Z final reply: Yo.",
  "dispatchResult": {
    "queuedFinal": true,
    "counts": {
      "tool": 0,
      "block": 0,
      "final": 0
    }
  },
  "sends": [
    {
      "kind": "final",
      "text": "PR89975-LIVE-PROOF-2026-06-07T02-41-20-545Z final reply: Yo.",
      "telegramMessageId": 4201
    }
  ],
  "toolMessagesSent": 0,
  "finalMessagesSent": 1
}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The exact-head live Telegram dispatcher attempted the failed progress payload `⚠️ 🛠️ PR89975-LIVE-PROOF-2026-06-07T02-41-20-545Z print lines 1-220 from memory/2026-06-03.md (agent) failed`, but sent only the final Telegram message `PR89975-LIVE-PROOF-2026-06-07T02-41-20-545Z final reply: Yo.` as Telegram message id `4201`. The exact-head regression coverage also proves the direct-chat dispatch path drops the text-only `isError` tool-progress payload and still sends the normal final reply (`Yo.`), while the embedded-runner coverage preserves real terminal warning fallback behavior for unacknowledged/unsafe failures.
- **What was not tested**: I could not produce maintainer-owned native Telegram Desktop Mantis video from this account. `workflow_dispatch` for `mantis-telegram-desktop-proof.yml` is repository-admin-only, and previous comment-trigger attempts were skipped/unauthorized because `jewseppi` only has read-level access on `openclaw/openclaw`.

## Test
- `pnpm test -- --run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`
- `pnpm test -- --run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`

## Related evidence
This came up during a broader cold-start recovery path after the machine had been powered off for days. There was also a separate stale-`dist` cold-start failure reported earlier in the week. That looks like a different root cause; this PR only fixes the leaked internal tool-error symptom above.

target.title: fix(gateway): log chat-abort terminal persistence failures
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #97795.

When the project session terminal persistence promise inside `registerChatAbortController` rejected, the `.catch()` block removed the abort controller without ever recording why. In production this silently masks DB or filesystem health failures during run cleanup — operators get no trace in telemetry or logs that a terminal state failed to persist.

## Why This Change Was Made

The bug is a missing telemetry signal, not a logic flaw. The existing behavior (remove the controller either way so memory does not leak) is correct; the only gap is observability. The fix adds a `gateway/chat-abort` subsystem `error` log on rejection carrying `runId` and the underlying error message, then keeps the existing cleanup behavior unchanged.

A subsystem logger is used (matching `src/gateway/boot.ts` and `src/gateway/channel-health-monitor.ts`) rather than the bare `logError` helper so the failure is attributable to this code path in structured logs.

## User Impact

- Operators can now observe chat-abort terminal persistence failures through the standard subsystem logger and correlate them with DB/filesystem health incidents.
- No behavioral change to run cleanup; abort controllers are still removed on persistence failure so memory does not leak.
- No gateway runtime or API contract change.

## Evidence

- New test in `src/gateway/chat-abort.test.ts`: "logs and still removes the controller when terminal persistence rejects". It registers a controller, attaches a rejecting `projectSessionTerminalPersistence`, calls `cleanup()`, and asserts both that the entry is removed and that the subsystem logger received `error` with the expected message and `{ runId, error }` metadata.
- Existing test "retains completed registrations until terminal persistence succeeds" still covers the success path.
- Local tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/chat-abort.test.ts` exits 0.
- Local typecheck: `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json` exits 0.

target.title: fix(codex): expose sandbox shell tools
target.body:
## Summary

- Expose OpenClaw sandbox-routed shell access to Codex as `sandbox_exec` and `sandbox_process` only for active non-Docker sandbox backends such as SSH.
- Keep Codex native shell enabled and keep plain OpenClaw `exec` / `process` filtered from Codex dynamic tools.
- Require both OpenClaw `exec` and `process` to be available before exposing the sandbox pair, and rewrite yielded `sandbox_exec` follow-up guidance to point at `sandbox_process`.
- Add regression coverage for backend gating, exclude handling, runtime allowlist mapping, missing-process fail-closed behavior, and yielded follow-up guidance.

Fixes #80322.

## Root Cause

PR #80048 correctly made Codex own native workspace tools by default, but the app-server dynamic-tool filter also removed the only OpenClaw shell path that honored `agents.list[].sandbox.backend` for SSH-backed sandbox sessions. Codex native shell remains the right default shell, but it does not represent OpenClaw's non-Docker sandbox backend routing.

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:changed`

`pnpm check:changed` result: Codex touched lanes passed through typecheck/lint/import-cycle setup, but the delegated Testbox run still fails on an unrelated existing extension-test type error:

```text
extensions/zalouser/src/zca-client.test.ts(20,19): error TS2339: Property 'options' does not exist on type ...
```

Latest changed-gate run: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/25657229446
