target.title: feat(agents): per-agent model allowlist (with fallback to global)
target.body:
When `agents.list[<agentId>].models` is defined and non-empty, scope the visibility allowlist (used by the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` catalog view) to that agent. Otherwise fall back to `agents.defaults.models` exactly as before.

Schema:
- `agents.list[].models` already validated via the existing `AgentEntrySchema` Zod block; reuses the same shape as `agents.defaults.models` (`alias`, `params`, `agentRuntime`, `streaming`).
- gateway protocol `models.list` params: optional `agentId` (NonEmptyString).

Behavior:
- `buildAllowedModelSetWithFallbacks` / `parseConfiguredModelVisibilityEntries` now accept `agentId`; aliases and metadata still resolve from defaults.
- gateway `server-methods/models` honors `models.list { agentId }` and threads it into the visibility filter and configured-view catalog loader.
- ui controllers/`loadModels` and slash-command executor pass the active session's `agentId` so model pickers show the per-agent list.

Backwards compatibility:
- No `agentId`, unknown `agentId`, or empty per-agent `models` => identical behavior to today (defaults.models drives the allowlist).
- `agents.defaults.models` semantics, aliases, and provider wildcards are unchanged. `agents.list[].models` entries can also use `provider/*` wildcards.

Tests:
- `src/agents/model-selection.test.ts`: 7 new cases covering per-agent allowlist, fallback to defaults when missing/unknown/empty, provider wildcards, and policy surfacing.
- `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts`: end-to-end visibility scoping via the public model-selection API.
- `src/gateway/server-methods/models.test.ts`: gateway `models.list` filter with `agentId`.
- `src/gateway/protocol/index.test.ts`: protocol schema accepts `agentId`.
- `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.test.ts`: client passes `agentId` through.
- `ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts` and `slash-command-executor.node.test.ts`: session-scoped catalog refresh forwards the agent id.

Docs:
- `docs/gateway/config-agents.md`: documents per-agent allowlist semantics, fallback rules, and an example config.

## Summary

- **Problem**: The visibility allowlist that powers the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` configured view is global — driven exclusively by `agents.defaults.models`. Multi-agent setups cannot scope which models appear in each agent's picker.
- **Why it matters**: With multi-agent setups (e.g. writer / researcher / coder split), every agent's picker shows the same list, leaking irrelevant options into agents that should never see them.
- **What changed**: `agents.list[<agentId>].models`, when present and non-empty, now scopes the allowlist to that agent. Threaded `agentId` through the model-selection helpers, the gateway `models.list` server method, the protocol schema, and the UI catalog loaders.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: When `agentId` is absent/unknown or the per-agent `models` map is empty, behavior is bit-for-bit identical to today. `agents.defaults.models` semantics, aliases, provider wildcards, and any other model resolution logic remain untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Today the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` configured view are driven exclusively by `agents.defaults.models` (global). With this change, when `agents.list[<agentId>].models` is defined and non-empty, the picker for that agent is scoped to that list; otherwise it falls back to the global allowlist exactly as before.
- **Real environment tested**: OpenClaw built locally from this branch (commit `5973f9d0c7`, version `2026.5.10-beta.1`) on Linux (WSL2), Node v22.22.2. Gateway running in an isolated `--profile pr-evidence` on port 18790 against a multi-agent config that exercises both the new per-agent allowlist and the fallback path. Productive install on the standard port left untouched.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran `node openclaw.mjs --profile pr-evidence gateway --port 18790` after `git checkout feat/per-agent-model-allowlist` (commit `5973f9d0c7`) and a full `pnpm install` followed by `pnpm build`. Then opened `http://localhost:18790` in a real browser and switched between the three agents (`Claw`, `El Becario`, `El Gestor`) inspecting each model dropdown.
- **Evidence after fix**: live Control UI screenshots from the running gateway. Before / after comparison embedded inline. Before, productive install on `2026.5.7` (no per-agent allowlist support), both agents render the same 9-model dropdown — Claw: ![claw-baseline](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/before-claw-dropdown.png) and El Becario: ![becario-baseline](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/before-becario-dropdown.png). After, built from this branch with per-agent `agents.list[].models` set on Claw (3 models) and El Becario (2 models), each dropdown is now scoped — Claw: ![claw-after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-claw-dropdown.png) and El Becario: ![becario-after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-becario-dropdown.png).
- **Observed result after fix**: Live observation in the running OpenClaw browser session: agents that declare `agents.list[].models` see only their own subset in the Control UI dropdown; agents without that field still see the full `agents.defaults.models` catalog. Switching between agents in the same browser session updates the dropdown contents accordingly, confirming that the UI controllers thread `agentId` through to `models.list` correctly. Concretely the Control UI rendered: `Claw` showed exactly its 3 declared models (`anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`, `ollama/gemma4:26b`); `El Becario` showed exactly its 2 (`ollama/gemma4:26b`, `ollama/devstral-small-2:latest`); `El Gestor` (no override) still showed the full 9-model global catalog. Live screenshot of Claw scoped picker: ![claw-after-2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-claw-dropdown.png) and live screenshot of El Becario scoped picker: ![becario-after-2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-becario-dropdown.png).
- **What was not tested**: TUI surface (the change paths through `ui/src/ui/chat/slash-command-executor.ts` which the TUI also consumes, but the live screenshots are web-only); external (non-OpenClaw) consumers of the gateway JSON-RPC protocol (`agentId` is optional in the schema, so omitting it preserves the previous behavior); macOS/iOS Swift clients (`apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawProtocol/GatewayModels.swift` was regenerated via `pnpm protocol:gen:swift` to include `agentid: String?` in `ModelsListParams`, but a live Swift consumer test was not run).

Config used (illustrative, redacted):

```jsonc
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "models": {
        "openai-codex/gpt-5.5": {},
        "ollama/gemma4:26b": {},
        "ollama/qwen3-coder:30b": {},
        "ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b": {},
        "ollama/devstral-small-2:latest": {},
        "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-opus-4-7": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-haiku-4-5": {}
      }
    },
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "main",
        "identity": { "name": "Claw" },
        "models": {
          "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7": {},
          "openai-codex/gpt-5.5": {},
          "ollama/gemma4:26b": {}
        }
      },
      {
        "id": "el-becario",
        "identity": { "name": "El Becario" },
        "models": {
          "ollama/gemma4:26b": {},
          "ollama/devstral-small-2:latest": {}
        }
      },
      { "id": "el-gestor", "identity": { "name": "El Gestor" } }
    ]
  }
}
```


## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature, not a regression.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — not a regression. New behavior is covered by the unit and integration tests listed above.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional config field: `agents.list[].models` (same shape as `agents.defaults.models`).
- Control UI model picker, `/model` command, and `models.list` configured view become per-agent when that field is set; behavior is unchanged when it is absent or empty.
- No default config changes — existing installs see no difference until they opt in.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
  [any agent] -> models.list (no agentId) -> defaults.models -> dropdown

After:
  [agent A with per-agent models] -> models.list { agentId: A } -> list[A].models -> dropdown
  [agent B without per-agent models] -> models.list { agentId: B } -> defaults.models (fallback) -> dropdown
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No** (the new field narrows visibility further; it never broadens access)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment
- OS: Linux (WSL2, kernel 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2)
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, OpenClaw 2026.5.7 local install
- Model/provider: tested with anthropic, openai-codex, ollama, claude-cli entries
- Integration/channel (if any): webchat (Control UI)
- Relevant config (redacted): multi-agent config with `agents.defaults.models` populated and one agent overriding via `agents.list[].models`.

### Steps
1. Apply patch, build, run tests.
2. Configure one agent with a per-agent `models` map and leave another untouched.
3. Switch agents in Control UI and inspect the model dropdown contents.

### Expected
- Agent with override: dropdown lists only the agent's models.
- Agent without override: dropdown lists `agents.defaults.models`.

### Actual
- Matches expected. All targeted test suites pass.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (new tests added; pre-existing suites unchanged)
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: agent-with-override shows scoped list; agent-without-override shows defaults; unknown `agentId` falls back to defaults; empty per-agent `models` falls back to defaults; provider wildcards (`provider/*`) work in both global and per-agent maps.
- **Edge cases checked**: missing `agents.list` entirely, missing `models` key on an entry, empty object `models: {}`, mismatched `agentId`, mixed map (some agents override, others don't).
- **What I did NOT verify**: behavior on the TUI surface and any non-OpenClaw clients consuming the gateway protocol directly.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I will reply to or resolve every bot review conversation I address in this PR.
- [x] I will leave unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes**
- Config/env changes? **No** (new field is purely additive and optional)
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: A user populates `agents.list[].models` expecting it to be additive on top of defaults.
  **Mitigation**: Documented in `docs/gateway/config-agents.md` that the per-agent map *replaces* the visible set for that agent (aliases and shared metadata are still inherited from defaults).

target.title: fix(gateway): disable startup trace monitor on startup failure
target.body:
## Summary
- add an explicit `startupTrace.close()` cleanup path in gateway startup tracing
- call `startupTrace.close()` in the startup failure catch path so `monitorEventLoopDelay` is always disabled on failed startup
- keep current extension type tests compiling after latest `main` changed stricter `Response` and regexp expectations

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Gateway startup tracing with `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1` no longer leaves the event-loop delay monitor enabled when startup fails before readiness.
- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux checkout of this PR branch at `e81371fe6a0bbc8a84d5c1a51a7a821611f268fa`, running Node through the repo's `tsx` loader against a real temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Created a temporary invalid `openclaw.json`, then ran:
  ```sh
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$tmpdir/openclaw.json" OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1 timeout 20s node --import tsx -e 'import("./src/gateway/server.impl.ts").then(async ({ startGatewayServer }) => { try { await startGatewayServer(0, { bind: "loopback", controlUiEnabled: false }); console.log("unexpected startup success"); process.exitCode = 2; } catch (error) { console.log("startup failed as expected"); console.log(String(error instanceof Error ? error.message : error).split("\n")[0]); } })'
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the after-fix run:
  ```text
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.file 0.5ms total=26.7ms eventLoopMax=9.9ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.hash 0.2ms total=47.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.parse 0.3ms total=49.3ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read 28.1ms total=53.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot 30.6ms total=55.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  startup failed as expected
  Invalid config at /tmp/openclaw-proof-4texI2/openclaw.json.
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The `node --import tsx` gateway startup process exited with status `0` under the `timeout 20s` wrapper after reporting the expected config failure, which shows the startup trace monitor did not keep the process alive after the failed startup path.
- **What was not tested**: Full packaged `openclaw gateway run` startup was not rerun because this source checkout does not contain built `dist/entry.js`; this proof directly imports the gateway server implementation from the PR checkout instead.

## Verification
- `corepack pnpm test src/gateway/server-channels.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/server.impl.ts extensions/googlechat/src/targets.test.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:test-types`

PR surface: Source +198, Tests +245. Total +443 across 3 files.

target.title: acp: cancel queued SessionActorQueue items on caller abort
target.body:
This PR is narrower than #52747 and targets a different failure mode:
it makes already-enqueued `SessionActorQueue` items cancellable on
caller abort, so `pendingBySession` does not stay inflated when the
predecessor tail never settles. Complementary to #52747's lane task
timeout work and #80239's Pi abort cleanup; does not add any config
or runtime timeout behavior.

## Summary

When a caller's `AbortSignal` fires before its actor reaches the head
of the per-key queue:

- the queued slot is marked cancelled (per-call closure flag),
- `pendingBySession` is decremented eagerly (idempotent, exactly once),
- the wrapped task throws `AbortError` instead of running `op()` when
  its turn arrives,
- per-key serialization invariant is preserved (later same-key tasks
  still wait for predecessor).

`withSessionActor` now forwards its existing signal to
`actorQueue.run(actorKey, op, { signal })` and the manager-side
abort-handling is simplified. The existing diagnostic log line
(`acp-manager: actor queue abort-before-start ...`) is preserved.

## Why

Without caller-side cancellation, `withSessionActor` could reject the
caller's promise on abort-before-start but had no way to remove or
cancel the already-enqueued item. If the predecessor tail did not
settle, the queued item could not reach `finally(onSettle)`, so
`pendingBySession` could not decrement and liveness diagnostics
reported `queued=N last=run:completed` for the actor key
indefinitely.

Sandbox repro reproduces the stale state in a few lines using a
non-settling first task, a same-key enqueue, an abort-before-start
on the second caller, and a measurement of `pendingBySession`.

## What changed

- `src/acp/control-plane/session-actor-queue.ts`: `run()` accepts an
  optional `signal: AbortSignal`. Per-call closure tracks `cancelled`,
  `actorStarted`, `pendingDecremented` flags. Signal listener
  decrements pending eagerly when actor has not started; the wrapped
  task throws `AbortError` when the cancelled slot reaches the queue
  head. Public Plugin SDK (`src/plugin-sdk/keyed-async-queue.ts`) is
  not changed — cancellation is implemented one level up.
- `src/acp/control-plane/manager.core.ts`: `withSessionActor` passes
  its existing signal to `actorQueue.run(actorKey, op, { signal })`.
  Manager-side `markAbortBeforeStart` call and the inline abort
  listener are no longer needed on this path; the queue takes over.
  Diagnostic log preserved verbatim.
- `src/acp/control-plane/session-actor-queue.test.ts`: 7 new tests
  covering signal-based cancellation, eager pending decrement,
  idempotent decrement, per-key serialization preservation,
  pre-aborted signal, queue still alive after cancelled item,
  backwards-compatibility for callers without signal. Existing
  diagnostic test preserved.

## Verification

pnpm test src/acp/control-plane/session-actor-queue.test.ts
✓ 8 passed

pnpm test src/acp/control-plane/session-actor-queue.test.ts
          src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts
✓ 64 passed

pnpm check:changed       ✓ EXIT 0
pnpm build               ✓ EXIT 0
pnpm exec oxfmt --check  ✓ clean

Live runtime validation on macOS gateway:
- Built from cherry-pick onto `v2026.5.6` tag
- Restarted gateway with patched `dist/` (smoke run 4.6s, status ok)
- Patched bundle contains `Aborted before start`, `pendingDecremented`,
  `actor queue abort-before-start` markers — fix loaded in memory
- 24h passive monitor observed no new
  `queued=N last=run:completed` stale liveness warnings

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Caller abort-before-start for already-enqueued `SessionActorQueue` items no longer leaves `pendingBySession` inflated when the predecessor tail never settles.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS live OpenClaw gateway setup, patched npm install built from cherry-pick onto `v2026.5.6`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Cherry-picked the source fix onto `v2026.5.6`, built, rsynced patched `dist/` into the live npm install, restarted the gateway, then ran a live smoke request against the restarted gateway process.
- **Evidence after fix:** Live output/runtime evidence from the patched setup: gateway restarted with pid `18313`; smoke run completed in `4.6s` with `status ok`; no Codex schema errors were observed. Patched bundle `dist/manager-D7F0BwP2.js` contains the after-fix markers `Aborted before start`, `pendingDecremented`, and `actor queue abort-before-start`. A 24h passive monitor is still running and has observed `0` stale queue warnings so far.
- **Observed result after fix:** The patched live gateway stayed up after restart, the smoke request succeeded, the patched queue cancellation markers were loaded in the running bundle, and passive monitoring has not seen new `queued=N last=run:completed` stale liveness warnings.
- **What was not tested:** Cross-OS live gateway validation was not repeated for this PR; local validation covered tests, changed checks, build, and formatter.

## Notes for reviewers

- This is intentionally separate from #52747 because the failure
  modes differ: #52747 targets a lane task that has *started but is
  stuck*, while this PR targets a caller cancellation *before the
  actor starts*. The two are complementary; either can be merged
  without conflicting with the other's intent, though both touch
  `session-actor-queue.ts` and `manager.core.ts` and will require a
  rebase of the second-merged PR.
- Public Plugin SDK is intentionally untouched. Backwards
  compatibility: `run(actorKey, op)` without `options` behaves
  identically to before.
- An earlier deployment experiment confirmed that `dist`-only
  patching across version lines is unsafe (Codex schema + Control
  UI mismatch). The fix in this PR is targeted at the source level
  and rebuilds cleanly on the same release line.
- May address parts of #13744 (session lock timeout) by reducing
  queue pressure that contributes to lock contention, though
  #13744's primary scope is the lock itself.

target.title: [codex] Further optimize Telegram media and reply context flow
target.body:
## Summary

- add scoped indexes to the Telegram message cache so `recentBefore` and `around` avoid scanning and sorting the full cache
- cache successful Telegram media resolutions by file identity and relevant runtime options
- revalidate cached media paths before reuse so TTL cleanup cannot leave stale attachments behind
- add regression coverage for thread-scoped chat windows, media cache hits, and stale media cache eviction
- bump `@openclaw/fs-safe` to 0.2.1 so focused Telegram tests can install and resolve `@openclaw/fs-safe/config` cleanly

## Context

This builds on earlier Telegram-route performance work by `obviyus`, further optimizing the same flow by tightening reply-context lookups and avoiding repeated Telegram media hydration.

## Why

Telegram reply-context building can repeatedly query recent chat windows and hydrate the same reply media. This PR keeps those hot paths bounded while preserving the existing chat-wide and thread-specific behavior.

The media cache also checks the saved file still exists before returning a cached path, because long-running gateways can remove saved media through TTL cleanup.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram reply-context lookups stay thread-scoped or chat-wide as requested, repeated Telegram media hydration reuses the cached saved path, and a deleted saved media file is evicted and re-downloaded instead of returning a stale path.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway from this PR head (`b8ad83191132ea2cdb1c9a40312ded58f58444ba`) on macOS, running `OpenClaw 2026.5.10-beta.1` with the Telegram plugin loaded from the PR checkout. Telegram ingress used the real Bot API webhook for `@acecoderbot` through a temporary Cloudflare tunnel. The LLM provider was mocked only to make the response deterministic; Telegram update delivery, reply-context hydration, message-cache persistence, and reply dispatch all ran through the PR checkout.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `pnpm openclaw --log-level debug gateway run --port 19043 --auth token --token [redacted] --bind loopback --verbose --ws-log compact` from the PR checkout with an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, a real Telegram Bot API webhook, and a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible provider. Sent `PR80243 live seed OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_1` to `@acecoderbot`, then sent a real Telegram reply to that DM with `PR80243 live reply-context OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_2`. Also ran a local media/cache proof through `node --import tsx` using a real temporary state directory and local trusted Bot API file path.
- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted live runtime logs and persisted cache/model-input excerpts from the actual PR gateway run:

target.title: feat(discord): add implicit reply mention policy
target.body:
Closes #80234.

Adds `channels.discord.implicitReplyMentions`:

```json5
{
  fromUsers: true,
  fromBots: true
}
```

Defaults preserve current behavior. Multi-bot deployments can set `fromBots: false` so bot-authored native replies do not wake another bot via the implicit `reply_to_bot` path; explicit `<@botId>` mentions still work.

Checks run:

- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.preflight.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Discord native reply-only mentions can wake an OpenClaw bot when another bot replies to one of its messages. This PR adds a policy knob so deployments can keep human reply activation while disabling bot-authored reply-only activation.

Real environment tested: Private Discord server with a private test channel and two OpenClaw Discord bot accounts. Server, channel, bot names, message IDs, and account IDs are intentionally omitted from this public proof.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. In the private test channel, send an anchor message from the target bot.
2. Send a native Discord reply from a separate relay bot to that anchor message. The reply body contains no textual `<@bot>` mention.
3. Observe whether the target bot wakes/responds.
4. Repeat after applying this branch with `implicitReplyMentions: { fromUsers: true, fromBots: false }`.
5. Send a separate control message from the relay bot that contains a real textual `<@bot>` mention and asks the target bot to reply with a known marker.

Evidence after fix: Redacted live output excerpt from the private Discord test channel:

```text
after-fix native reply-only test:
relay bot | "oc-proof-80235-after4-231018 native-reply-no-explicit-mention" | ref=<target-bot-anchor>
UNEXPECTED_TARGET_RESPONSES=0

explicit mention control:
target bot | "oc-proof-80235-control-231107 explicit-mention-ok" | ref=<relay-bot-control-message>
EXPLICIT_CONTROL_OK=true
```

Observed result after fix: With `fromBots:false`, the bot-authored native reply-only message did not wake the target bot during the observation window. A real textual explicit mention still woke the target bot and produced the requested marker response.

What was not tested: Human-authored reply-only behavior was covered by unit tests but not repeated in the live Discord proof. PluralKit behavior was covered by unit tests but not repeated in the live Discord proof.

target.title: [codex] Add Codex continuity bridge
target.body:
## Summary

Adds the Codex Continuity Bridge to the bundled Codex plugin.

The bridge keeps Codex UI as the high-bandwidth workbench while giving OpenClaw a quiet operator surface for Telegram: status, recent threads, explicit watched completion checks, evidence-separated handoff briefs, and a gated write path that is disabled by default.

## Safety Model

- Read-only Codex app-server status is preferred.
- SQLite fallback is read-only and explicitly stale-labeled.
- Raw Codex app-server blobs are not exposed by the public HTTP routes.
- Watch notifications are explicit, deduped, redacted, and quiet by default.
- Telegram-to-Codex writes require an explicit feature flag plus provenance, trusted sender checks, repo allowlisting, risk gating, unambiguous target selection, and confirmed app-server write methods.
- SQLite is never used as a write path.

## Validation

- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/codex/tsconfig.json --noEmit`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/codex/src/continuity/bridge.test.ts extensions/codex/src/commands.test.ts` (89 tests)
- Local gateway smoke test for `/codex/status`, `/codex/threads`, `/codex/watch`, `/codex/watch/check`, `/codex/handoff`, and `/codex/goal`

target.title: fix: propagate gateway TLS fingerprints to bootstrap clients
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Gateway clients bootstrapped outside `callGateway` (ACP, MCP channel bridge, and native approval handling) did not receive the local TLS certificate fingerprint for a self-signed local WSS gateway.
- Why it matters: those clients could fail TLS verification even when the local gateway bootstrap had enough information to trust the generated certificate.
- What changed: added a shared TLS fingerprint resolver, reused it from `callGateway`, propagated `bootstrap.tlsFingerprint` into ACP/MCP/approval `GatewayClient` construction, and added regression coverage for local, remote, override, and non-TLS cases.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway server TLS behavior, auth mode, config schema, environment variable, or public endpoint changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes: N/A
- Related: N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: ACP bootstrap can reach a locally configured TLS gateway using the bootstrapped local TLS fingerprint instead of failing certificate verification.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, temporary config/state under `/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke`, self-signed local WSS gateway on `127.0.0.1:19791`, auth disabled for the smoke.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/state \
pnpm openclaw gateway run --allow-unconfigured --port 19791 --ws-log compact

OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/state \
pnpm openclaw gateway health --url wss://127.0.0.1:19791

printf 'exit\n' | \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/state \
pnpm openclaw acp client --cwd /home/jet/openclaw-dev
```

- Evidence after fix (copied terminal/log output):

```text
Gateway Health
OK (27ms)

[gateway] http server listening ...
[gateway] ready
[ws] ⇄ res ✓ health ...

ACP output reached the gateway connection path and then stopped at the expected local scope approval gate:
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 84eb4a78-9285-4ccf-8ee1-8ba98c5945a5)
ACP bridge failed: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 84eb4a78-9285-4ccf-8ee1-8ba98c5945a5)
```

- Observed result after fix: local TLS health succeeded, and ACP reached the gateway authorization path without a TLS/self-signed certificate or fingerprint mismatch.
- What was not tested: a full ACP prompt turn after approving the scope upgrade in the temporary local gateway.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the bug was covered by the new regression tests rather than a saved failing runtime log.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: local TLS fingerprint selection lived inside `callGateway`, while shared bootstrap resolution only returned URL/auth/timeout data. ACP, MCP channel bridge, and operator approval clients constructed `GatewayClient` without the local TLS fingerprint.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no test asserted that bootstrap clients forward a resolved TLS fingerprint into `GatewayClient`.
- Contributing context (if known): remote-mode and environment URL overrides needed explicit precedence rules so remote pins are applied only to TLS remote targets.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/client-bootstrap.test.ts`, `src/acp/server.startup.test.ts`, `src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: local TLS bootstrap returns and forwards the local fingerprint; explicit CLI fingerprints win; remote TLS fingerprints are inherited only for TLS remote targets; non-TLS targets do not receive TLS pins.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it validates the shared resolver and each client construction seam without needing a long-lived gateway process.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `src/gateway/call.test.ts` covered `callGateway` behavior only.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Local self-signed TLS gateway clients launched through ACP, MCP channel bridge, and native approval handling now inherit the same trusted fingerprint behavior that `callGateway` already had.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
resolve bootstrap -> GatewayClient(url, token) -> local WSS cert verification can fail

After:
resolve bootstrap -> resolve TLS fingerprint -> GatewayClient(url, token, tlsFingerprint) -> local WSS trust succeeds
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux local development machine
- Runtime/container: Node/pnpm local checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway local WSS, ACP client smoke
- Relevant config (redacted): temporary config with `gateway.mode=local`, `gateway.tls.enabled=true`, `gateway.tls.autoGenerate=true`, `gateway.bind=loopback`, `gateway.port=19791`

### Steps

1. Start a temporary local TLS gateway with self-signed auto-generated certs.
2. Run `pnpm openclaw gateway health --url wss://127.0.0.1:19791` against that gateway.
3. Start `pnpm openclaw acp client --cwd /home/jet/openclaw-dev` against the same temporary config.
4. Run targeted gateway/ACP/MCP regression tests and changed-file checks.
5. Run Codex review against `origin/main` and address the findings.

### Expected

- Bootstrap clients receive the correct TLS fingerprint for the selected gateway target.
- Local WSS bootstrap does not fail due to the self-signed gateway certificate.
- Remote TLS pins are not applied to plain `ws://` targets or local fallback targets.

### Actual

- `gateway health` returned `OK` over local WSS.
- ACP reached the gateway authorization path and stopped at the expected scope upgrade approval gate, with no TLS/fingerprint failure.
- Targeted tests and changed-file checks passed.
- Codex review reported no remaining actionable correctness issues after two review fixes.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Validation commands run locally:

```bash
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test \
  src/gateway/client-bootstrap.test.ts \
  src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts \
  src/gateway/call.test.ts \
  src/acp/server.startup.test.ts \
  src/mcp/channel-server.test.ts

pnpm check:changed
git diff --check
codex review --base origin/main
```

Results:

```text
3 Vitest shards, 114 tests passed
pnpm check:changed passed
git diff --check passed
codex review --base origin/main: No actionable correctness issues were found
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local TLS fingerprint selection, explicit fingerprint precedence, remote WSS fingerprint inheritance, `ws://` remote target exclusion, remote-mode local fallback choosing local TLS fingerprint, ACP/MCP/approval client propagation seams.
- Edge cases checked: CLI override vs config-derived URL, env URL override, remote WSS, remote plain WS, missing remote URL fallback, no TLS config.
- What you did **not** verify: a full ACP prompt turn after manually approving the temporary local scope upgrade.

AI-assisted implementation note: this patch was implemented with OpenClaw/Codex assistance, and Codex review was run locally before PR creation.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: applying a remote TLS fingerprint to the wrong target could break local fallback or non-TLS gateway connections.
  - Mitigation: the shared resolver only applies remote fingerprints to selected TLS remote targets, with regression tests for `ws://` and missing-remote-url fallback behavior.

target.title: Prioritize foreground command queue work
target.body:
Summary:
- Add Low/Normal/High priority scheduling inside command lanes while preserving FIFO order within a priority.
- Promote old low/normal entries after the starvation threshold so background work cannot wait forever.
- Mark user/manual embedded runs high priority and cron/heartbeat/memory/deferred maintenance work low priority, with docs and changelog coverage.

Real behavior proof:
- Foreground user/manual turns that are queued behind lower-priority cron, heartbeat, memory, or deferred maintenance work in the same lane now run first when the lane next has capacity. Active tasks are not interrupted.
- Regression coverage proves high-priority dequeue, FIFO within a priority, starvation promotion, and legacy/malformed priority normalization for preserved singleton queue entries.

Verification:
- `pnpm test src/process/command-queue.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/context-engine-maintenance.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/process/command-queue.test.ts`

target.title: fix(acp): reject fractional runtime timeouts
target.body:
## Summary
- reject fractional numeric ACP runtime timeout patches instead of silently rounding them
- add regression coverage for fractional `timeoutSeconds` values

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** ACP runtime option validation should reject fractional numeric `timeoutSeconds` values instead of silently rounding them. Integer seconds should still be accepted.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS after `pnpm install`, branch `fix/acp-runtime-timeout-integer`, commit `571ad9f7d6`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a live Node command with `tsx` import support against the local checkout; called `validateRuntimeOptionPatch` with `1.4`, `1.6`, and integer `2`.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the live command:

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "import { validateRuntimeOptionPatch } from './src/acp/control-plane/runtime-options.ts'; for (const value of [1.4, 1.6]) { try { console.log(value, validateRuntimeOptionPatch({ timeoutSeconds: value })); } catch (error) { console.log(value, error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error)); } } console.log('integer', JSON.stringify(validateRuntimeOptionPatch({ timeoutSeconds: 2 })));"
1.4 Timeout must be a positive integer in seconds.
1.6 Timeout must be a positive integer in seconds.
integer {"timeoutSeconds":2}
```

**Observed result after fix:** Fractional numeric timeout patches now fail with `Timeout must be a positive integer in seconds.`, while an integer timeout patch still returns `{ "timeoutSeconds": 2 }`.

**What was not tested:** Full live ACP client session. The proof exercises the actual runtime-options module in a real local OpenClaw checkout; unit and changed-scope checks cover the integration surface.

## Test Plan
- `pnpm test src/acp/control-plane/runtime-options.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Notes
The string/config-option path already requires integer seconds via `parseRuntimeTimeoutSecondsInput`; this aligns the numeric runtime patch path with that contract.

target.title: fix: add resilient fallback policy for user model overrides
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: session/user model overrides always disabled configured fallback chains.
- Why it matters: operators could configure provider fallback behavior, but a user-selected model had no fallback safety net during transient provider/model failures.
- What changed: adds opt-in `userOverrideFallbackPolicy: "resilient"` on `agents.defaults.model` and per-agent chat model config, with schema/types/help/labels/docs and fallback-resolution tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): default behavior remains `strict`; auto-selected model overrides and configured agent primaries keep their existing fallback behavior; non-chat model config schemas remain unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: a user/session model override can now inherit the configured fallback chain when the new policy is explicitly set to `resilient`, while the default `strict` behavior still returns no fallbacks.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on pop-os, Node v25.8.1, branch `fix/user-override-fallback-policy`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```bash
  pnpm exec tsx -e 'import { resolveEffectiveModelFallbacks } from "./src/agents/agent-scope.ts"; const cfg={agents:{defaults:{model:{primary:"openai/gpt-5.4",fallbacks:["anthropic/claude-haiku-3-5","google/gemini-2.5-flash"],userOverrideFallbackPolicy:"resilient"}},list:[{id:"linus"}]}}; console.log("user override resilient =>", JSON.stringify(resolveEffectiveModelFallbacks({cfg,agentId:"linus",hasSessionModelOverride:true,modelOverrideSource:"user"}))); const strict={agents:{defaults:{model:{primary:"openai/gpt-5.4",fallbacks:["anthropic/claude-haiku-3-5"]}},list:[{id:"linus"}]}}; console.log("user override strict =>", JSON.stringify(resolveEffectiveModelFallbacks({cfg:strict,agentId:"linus",hasSessionModelOverride:true,modelOverrideSource:"user"})));'
  ```
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  ```text
  user override resilient => ["anthropic/claude-haiku-3-5","google/gemini-2.5-flash"]
  user override strict => []
  ```
- Observed result after fix: resilient user overrides receive the configured fallback list; strict user overrides receive an empty fallback list.
- What was not tested: live provider failover against real external model APIs, because that would require changing a running gateway config. The changed resolver and config schema were tested locally.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): covered by the existing and new regression tests: the pre-change resolver returned `[]` for user overrides whenever `hasSessionModelOverride` was true.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: `resolveEffectiveModelFallbacks` treated every non-auto session model override as authoritative and returned an empty fallback list without a config escape hatch.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression coverage for user-selected models that should intentionally keep operator-configured fallback chains.
- Contributing context (if known): auto model overrides and user/session overrides were represented in the same resolver path, but operators need separate fallback policy for user choices.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts`, `src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts`

target.title: fix(browser): time out remote tab enumeration
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes #58968 by bounding remote CDP Playwright tab enumeration with the existing remote CDP timeout budget.
- Evicts the scoped cached/in-flight Playwright connection on enumeration timeout so the next request can reconnect instead of reusing a stuck pipe.
- Adds regression coverage for a stuck page-target lookup and verifies remote profile tab listing passes the resolved timeout into the Playwright path.

## Problem
Remote CDP profiles use a persistent Playwright connection for tab listing because HTTP `/json/list` is ephemeral for those providers. When that Playwright-backed enumeration stalls, browser requests can hang behind the stuck CDP operation instead of failing fast.

## Root Cause
`createProfileTabOps().readTabs()` passed only `cdpUrl` and SSRF policy to `listPagesViaPlaywright()`. The lower-level Playwright enumeration path had no operation-level timeout around `connectBrowser()`, `getAllPages()`, or per-page target/title/url reads, and a stuck scoped connection could remain cached or in-flight.

## Architectural Reasoning
The fix reuses the existing browser remote CDP timeout contract rather than adding a new config key. Remote tab listing now uses `max(remoteCdpTimeoutMs, remoteCdpHandshakeTimeoutMs)`, which matches the existing distinction between HTTP discovery and WebSocket handshake budgets. Timeout cleanup goes through the existing scoped Playwright disconnect helper, preserving per-CDP URL isolation and avoiding hidden coupling to other browser profiles.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Remote CDP Playwright tab enumeration now returns a bounded timeout error instead of waiting indefinitely on a stalled CDP discovery/enumeration path.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 worktree, Node `v22.15.0`, `pnpm 10.33.2`, branch `hemant/issue-triage-20260509`, commit `9aba9b405ae5c4601211fd0b2b428d9640425b8b`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Started a real loopback HTTP endpoint that accepts CDP discovery connections and deliberately never responds; called production `listPagesViaPlaywright()` through `node --import tsx` with `timeoutMs: 100` and an SSRF policy allowing loopback/private access; closed the scoped Playwright connection and destroyed the held socket after the call.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```console
$ node --import tsx - < hanging-cdp-smoke.mjs
{"ok":true,"error":"Playwright page enumeration timed out after 100ms","elapsedMs":309}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The production browser module returned `Playwright page enumeration timed out after 100ms` and the script exited cleanly instead of hanging on the held CDP request.
- **What was not tested:** I did not use a real remote Chrome host because the failure mode is a stalled CDP endpoint; the local loopback endpoint exercises the same production timeout path with deterministic cleanup. Full `pnpm test extensions/browser` still has unrelated Windows environment failures noted below.

## Testing
- `pnpm test extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.connections.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.remote-profile-tab-ops.playwright.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.tab-ops.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.connections.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.remote-profile-tab-ops.playwright.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions` from `O:\` subst drive to avoid the repo path-with-spaces launcher bug
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` from `O:\`
- `pnpm lint:extensions` from `O:\`
- `pnpm check:changelog-attributions`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `pnpm check:changed --staged` exited 0 with no lane output

Full `pnpm test extensions/browser` was also run locally. It failed on unrelated Windows environment assumptions: local browser auto-detection / missing supported browser launch fixtures and existing `/tmp` path expectations being normalized to `C:\tmp`. The two touched regression test files pass.

## Risk Analysis
Risk is medium because this touches async browser/CDP lifecycle behavior. The change is scoped to remote Playwright tab enumeration, does not alter local managed browser JSON tab listing, and does not change navigation, action, or screenshot behavior. On timeout, OpenClaw drops only the scoped Playwright connection for that CDP URL and leaves the actual remote browser tabs intact.

## Backward Compatibility
No config, API, or response-shape changes. Existing `browser.remoteCdpTimeoutMs` and `browser.remoteCdpHandshakeTimeoutMs` now also bound remote Playwright tab enumeration.

## Screenshots / Logs
The real behavior proof above is terminal output from the after-fix smoke; no UI screenshot is applicable.

target.title: fix(skills): warn for untrusted local sources
target.body:
Fixes #57535

## Problem summary
Local skills from workspace, `.agents`, managed, and extra skill roots were shown as ordinary ready/blocked skills. Operators had no first-class status signal distinguishing ClawHub/bundled skills from local skill files that should be reviewed before enablement or invocation.

## Root cause
The skills loader already tracks each skill source, and ClawHub installs already write `.clawhub/origin.json`, but `buildWorkspaceSkillStatus` collapsed that into `source`/`bundled` only. CLI and Control UI status surfaces therefore had no trust metadata to render.

## Architectural reasoning
This keeps runtime loading semantics unchanged and adds trust classification at the status/reporting layer. Built-in skills are trusted as `openclaw-bundled`; valid ClawHub origin metadata is trusted as `clawhub`; reviewed local roots configured in `skills.load.trustedDirs` are trusted as `trusted-dir`; known local sources without either signal are marked `untrustedLocalSource` with a warning. The change is additive to the status API and avoids coupling core loader behavior to owner-specific skill ids.

## Testing performed
- `pnpm test src/agents/skills-status.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm test src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm test src/commands/doctor-skills.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/agents/skills-status.ts src/agents/skills-status.test.ts src/commands/doctor-skills.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.format.ts src/cli/skills-cli.test.ts src/config/types.skills.ts src/config/zod-schema.ts src/config/schema.help.ts src/config/schema.labels.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts ui/src/ui/types.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills-shared.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills.test.ts docs/tools/skills.md docs/tools/skills-config.md` passed.
- `pnpm lint:core` passed.
- `pnpm lint:docs` passed.
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` passed via a temporary `P:\` mapping to avoid the repo path-with-spaces issue.
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test` passed via the same temporary mapping.
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui` passed via the same temporary mapping.
- `pnpm config:docs:check` initially detected expected config baseline drift; after `pnpm config:docs:gen`, `pnpm config:docs:check` passed.
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD` passed after clean rebase.
- `pnpm check:changed` passed locally. Blacksmith Testbox was unavailable because `blacksmith` is not installed on PATH.

`pnpm test:changed` was also run locally and selected a broad `unit-fast` shard; it failed in 19 unrelated files with existing Windows/path/host assumptions such as `/tmp` becoming `C:\tmp`, POSIX table glyph expectations on Windows output, A2UI scaffold 404 in `extensions/canvas`, and temp SQLite cleanup `EBUSY`. The focused touched-surface tests above passed.

`pnpm format:docs:check` could not be used as proof in this environment because the wrapper crashed on Node v22.15.0 with `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'trim')` in `scripts/format-docs.mjs`; targeted `oxfmt --check`, `lint:docs`, and `docs:check-mdx` passed.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: A temporary workspace skill without ClawHub origin metadata is now classified as an untrusted local source.
- Real environment tested: Windows checkout on branch `hemant/57535-untrusted-skill-sources`, Node v22.15.0, pnpm 10.33.2.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a `pnpm exec tsx` script from the repo root that created a temporary `<workspace>/skills/local-review/SKILL.md`, called `buildWorkspaceSkillStatus(workspace)`, and rendered `formatSkillsCheck(report, {})`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied terminal output from the local run:

```json
{
  "name": "local-review",
  "source": "openclaw-workspace",
  "trustSource": "local",
  "untrustedLocalSource": true,
  "hasWarning": true
}
```

```text
Untrusted local sources
local-review
Add the directory to skills.load.trustedDirs only if you trust this workspace-owned skill source.
```

- Observed result after fix: Local skill loading still succeeded and the status/check surface warned before users enable or invoke the local skill.
- What was not tested: No browser screenshot was captured because this is a status/API/CLI/UI-rendering change covered by jsdom UI tests rather than a visual layout change.

## Risk analysis
Low-to-medium. The classification is additive and synchronous, using small local origin/trusted-dir checks during status generation. It does not alter skill discovery, precedence, eligibility, model visibility, command visibility, or execution. Main residual risk is users seeing new warnings for legitimate local/plugin-provided skill roots until they add reviewed directories to `skills.load.trustedDirs`.

## Backward compatibility
Backward-compatible. Existing skill config continues to load the same skills. New `SkillStatusEntry` fields are additive, and `skills.load.trustedDirs` is optional. Existing `skills.load.allowSymlinkTargets` behavior is unchanged; docs now clarify that it controls loader containment while `trustedDirs` controls warning suppression.

## Screenshots/logs
No screenshots. Relevant command output is summarized above.

target.title: fix(gateway): add systemd watchdog heartbeat
target.body:
## Problem summary

Closes #11973. The generated systemd gateway unit had crash restart behavior, but it did not configure a watchdog heartbeat. If the gateway event loop wedged without exiting, systemd had no liveness signal to trigger recovery.

## Root cause

The existing gateway maintenance loop already emits a 30s keepalive tick, but that liveness signal only reaches OpenClaw clients. The systemd unit renderer did not opt into `WatchdogSec`, and the gateway runtime did not notify systemd through the `NOTIFY_SOCKET`/`systemd-notify` contract.

## Architectural reasoning

- Added a small `src/infra/systemd-notify.ts` helper that is inert unless `NOTIFY_SOCKET` is present, so non-systemd, Windows, macOS, foreground, and unmanaged gateway runs keep the same behavior.
- Reused `startGatewayMaintenanceTimers` as the watchdog cadence source. This ties the systemd ping to the existing event-loop tick instead of creating a separate interval or lifecycle path.
- Kept the systemd unit on the existing default `Type=simple` behavior and only added `WatchdogSec=90s` plus `NotifyAccess=all`. The `NotifyAccess=all` line is needed because notifications are sent by the `systemd-notify` helper process inside the service cgroup.
- The notifier disables itself after the first `systemd-notify` failure and logs once, avoiding repeated subprocess failures while preserving the gateway's existing session, cleanup, and health loops.
- No gateway protocol, API, session, or backward-compatibility contract changes.

## Testing performed

- `pnpm test src/infra/systemd-notify.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts` passed after rebase: 3 Vitest shards, 19 tests.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/systemd-notify.ts src/infra/systemd-notify.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts docs/gateway/index.md docs/platforms/linux.md docs/vps.md CHANGELOG.md` passed.
- `pnpm lint:core` passed.
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx` passed.
- `pnpm lint:docs -- docs/gateway/index.md docs/platforms/linux.md docs/vps.md` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:core:test` passed from a subst drive path to avoid the local Windows path-with-space issue.
- `pnpm check:changed --staged` and `pnpm check:changed` exited 0 in this checkout, though they produced no lane output.

Known local environment limitations:

- Adding `src/daemon/systemd.test.ts` to the focused test command still shows pre-existing Windows-only failures unrelated to this change: `process.geteuid` is absent on Windows, and two env-file assertions expect an unquoted Windows path. The changed renderer test itself passes.
- `pnpm build` could not be completed in this Windows shell. The default `pnpm` wrapper is pinned to local Node v22.15.0 while the repo requires `>=22.16.0`; a subst-drive retry with bundled Node v24.14.0 gets past that but `build-all` still fails at the bootstrap guard because the expected generated `.js` dist aliases are missing in this environment. A direct `tsdown` build completed successfully, and generated hash drift was restored.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: Generated systemd gateway units now include watchdog recovery settings, and the runtime notifier is a safe no-op outside systemd-managed services.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows, branch `hemant/11973-systemd-watchdog`, Node v22.15.0 for focused proof commands.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `pnpm exec tsx` commands from the repo root to render the generated systemd unit and invoke the notifier with no `NOTIFY_SOCKET`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied terminal output from the local run:

```console
$ pnpm exec tsx -e "import { buildSystemdUnit } from './src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts'; const unit = buildSystemdUnit({ description: 'OpenClaw Gateway', programArguments: ['/usr/bin/openclaw','gateway','run'], environment: {} }); console.log(unit.split('\n').filter((line) => ['Restart=always','RestartSec=5','WatchdogSec=90s','NotifyAccess=all','KillMode=control-group'].includes(line)).join('\n'));"
Restart=always
RestartSec=5
WatchdogSec=90s
NotifyAccess=all
KillMode=control-group

$ pnpm exec tsx -e "import { createSystemdNotifier } from './src/infra/systemd-notify.ts'; void (async () => { const notifier = createSystemdNotifier({ env: {} }); await notifier.ready(); await notifier.watchdog(); console.log('systemd notifier no-op without NOTIFY_SOCKET ok'); })();"
systemd notifier no-op without NOTIFY_SOCKET ok
```

- Observed result after fix: The actual unit renderer includes the watchdog lines, and the notifier performs no work when the systemd notification socket is absent. Unmanaged/local gateway runs remain unaffected, while managed systemd units can receive liveness pings from the existing maintenance tick.
- What was not tested: A live Linux systemd user service restart on a hung event loop from this Windows workspace.

## Risk analysis

Medium-low. The unit settings affect systemd-managed gateway installs only. Runtime notifications are gated by `NOTIFY_SOCKET`, failure-disabled after the first subprocess error, and do not change gateway protocol/session flows. The main operational risk is a missing `systemd-notify` binary on a systemd host; in that case the helper logs once and leaves existing crash-restart behavior intact.

## Screenshots/logs

No UI screenshots. This is CLI/service generation behavior; relevant command output is included above.

## Backward compatibility

Backward compatible. Existing unmanaged gateway runs, existing installed units until regenerated, non-systemd platforms, gateway API behavior, and session/gateway protocol behavior are unchanged. Docs now show the watchdog lines alongside the generated unit settings.

target.title: fix(tls): add subjectAltName to auto-generated gateway self-signed certificate (fixes #96910)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #96910.

When gateway TLS is enabled with `autoGenerate=true`, the self-signed certificate had no `subjectAltName` extension. Node.js ≥17 ignores the CN for hostname/IP verification per RFC 6125, causing CLI probe to `wss://127.0.0.1:18789` to fail.

## Changes Made

- New certs: Added `-addext subjectAltName=DNS:localhost,DNS:<hostname>,IP:127.0.0.1,IP:::1` to the openssl command
- Existing certs: Auto-detect CN-only certs (`CN=openclaw-gateway`, no SAN) and regenerate with SANs
- Transactional upgrade: old files renamed to `.bak`, restored on failure
- Custom certs (non-OpenClaw CN) are never touched

## Evidence

- **Unit tests:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/tls/gateway.test.ts` — 10/10 passed

## Real behavior proof

```
# New cert: SAN verified via openssl
$ openssl x509 -in gateway-cert.pem -noout -ext subjectAltName
X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
    DNS:localhost, DNS:LIN-66D8BD4EA2C, IP Address:127.0.0.1, IP Address:0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1

# Strict TLS check passes
$ node -e "tls.checkServerIdentity('127.0.0.1', cert)"
✅ STRICT TLS CHECK PASSED for 127.0.0.1

# Upgrade path: CN-only cert auto-detected and regenerated
[warn] gateway tls: upgrading CN-only certificate to include subjectAltName
[info] gateway tls: generated self-signed cert at ...

# Custom cert not touched
BEFORE: CN=custom-server, no SAN
AFTER:  CN=custom-server, no SAN (unchanged)
```

- ✅ New certs include subjectAltName
- ✅ Strict TLS identity check passes for IP and hostname
- ✅ Existing CN-only certs auto-upgraded with transactional backup/rollback
- ✅ Custom certificates (non-OpenClaw) are never rewritten

- [x] AI-assisted

target.title: fix(agents): repair orphaned tool_use pairs on compaction prune path
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #93321

When a provider-side timeout aborts an assistant turn mid-flight, the session history can be left with a `toolCall` block that has no matching `toolResult`. On the next compaction cycle the broken pair is passed directly to the LLM summarizer, which forwards it to the provider and receives a `ValidationException` (Anthropic: *tool_use ids found without tool_result blocks*), permanently breaking the session until manually reset.

## Root Cause

`repairToolUseResultPairing()` was only called in two places:
1. `splitPreservedRecentTurns()` — repairs the **no-drop** summarizable slice
2. `compaction-safeguard.ts` drop path — repairs **dropped** messages (for the dropped-chunk summary)

It was **not** called on `pruned.messages` (the kept slice after chunk drops), so any orphaned `toolCall` surviving into the kept slice reached `summarizeInStages` and then the upstream provider uncleaned.

Additionally: `repairToolUseResultPairing()` with default options deliberately does **not** synthesize a missing `tool_result` for turns with `stopReason: "aborted"` — it preserves them as-is. The fix passes `{ erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop" }` to drop the entire aborted turn (including its unpaired `toolCall`), matching what already happens in the prompt-send sanitizer path.

## Fix

**`compaction-safeguard.ts`** — apply `repairToolUseResultPairing()` with `erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop"` to `pruned.messages` before assigning to `messagesToSummarize`:

```diff
- messagesToSummarize = pruned.messages;
+ messagesToSummarize = repairToolUseResultPairing(pruned.messages, {
+   erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop",
+ }).messages;
```

Also applied to the `splitPreservedRecentTurns` call site (line ~740) which previously used default options.

**`compaction-planning.ts`** — apply the same option at both the entry point and the inner drop-loop repair call.

## Real Session Evidence

**Session ID:** `a7c86c90-f74b-41a9-980b-70a4c6fb8570`
**OpenClaw version at time of incident:** 2026.5.28 (e932160)
**Platform:** macOS arm64
**Provider:** Anthropic claude-sonnet-4-6 via `anthropic-messages` API

### Exact JSONL records from the broken session

**L154 — orphaned assistant turn (aborted mid-flight):**
```json
{
  "type": "message",
  "id": "f5809271",
  "timestamp": "2026-06-15T09:04:13.833Z",
  "message": {
    "role": "assistant",
    "content": [
      {
        "type": "toolCall",
        "id": "toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c",
        "name": "message",
        "arguments": { "action": "send", "message": "[REDACTED]" }
      }
    ]
  }
}
```
*(thinking block omitted for brevity; `stopReason` absent — turn was never completed)*

**L155 — provider timeout event (63ms after L154):**
```json
{
  "type": "custom",
  "customType": "openclaw:prompt-error",
  "data": {
    "runId": "5ea7792a-18bd-4a56-9903-d8441437de78",
    "sessionId": "a7c86c90-f74b-41a9-980b-70a4c6fb8570",
    "provider": "anthropic",
    "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
    "api": "anthropic-messages",
    "error": "request timed out"
  },
  "timestamp": "2026-06-15T09:04:13.896Z"
}
```

**L157 — fallback gpt-5 turn with empty content (no `toolResult` for `toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c`):**
```json
{
  "type": "message",
  "timestamp": "2026-06-15T09:04:14.281Z",
  "message": {
    "role": "assistant",
    "content": [],
    "api": "openai-completions",
    "provider": "openai-gateway",
    "model": "gpt-5",
    "stopReason": "stop"
  }
}
```

### Permanent failure — every subsequent turn for 8 hours:
```
L160  2026-06-15T09:36:56  assistant: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
L162  2026-06-15T09:37:10  assistant: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
L164  2026-06-15T09:52:59  assistant: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
L166  2026-06-15T09:55:13  assistant: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
L168  2026-06-15T09:57:57  assistant: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
L170  2026-06-15T10:29:38  assistant: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
```
Session unresponsive from 09:04 until manual reset — confirmed `ValidationException: tool_use ids found without tool_result blocks` sent to Anthropic on every API call.

### Why the fix resolves this

With `erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop"`, `repairToolUseResultPairing()` drops the entire aborted assistant turn at compaction time. The broken `toolCall id=toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c` is removed before the message sequence reaches `summarizeInStages` or the upstream provider. The next API call is valid and the session recovers.

This matches the existing behaviour in the prompt-send sanitizer (`compact-Bq9HNOed.js`) which already passes `erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop"` — the compaction path was the only place missing this guard.

## After-Fix Replay — Real Session Data

**Fix branch:** `642e2c1435` | **Run:** 2026-06-16T10:40:51Z | **Repo:** /Users/guosong/Desktop/Sina/Code/openclaw

Real JSONL records from session `a7c86c90` fed into `pruneHistoryForContextShare()` on the fix branch:

```
Input:
  [0] role=user    content=[这个问题帮我看看]
  [1] role=assistant content=[thinking, toolCall:toolu_..._8f2aa84c]  ← ORPHANED
  [2] role=assistant content=[]  model=gpt-5  stopReason=stop
  [3] role=user    content=[xxxxx... 20000 chars]
  [4] role=user    content=[xxxxx... 20000 chars]
  [5] role=user    content=[xxxxx... 20000 chars]

Output (droppedChunks=2):
  [0] role=user content=[xxxxx...]

Validation:
  Orphaned toolCall blocks remaining: 0
  ✅ PASS: toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c dropped
           — compaction would NOT send invalid transcript to provider
```

The aborted turn is removed by `repairToolUseResultPairing({ erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop" })` before reaching `summarizeInStages` or the upstream provider.

## Tests

- **`compaction-safeguard.test.ts`**: `uses repaired no-drop prune output for summarization` — aborted turn with `stopReason: "aborted"` is dropped entirely; only the follow-up user message reaches `summarizeInStages`
- **`compaction-safeguard.test.ts`**: `repairs orphaned tool_use in pruned.messages when chunks are dropped` — no unpaired `toolCall` reaches `summarizeInStages` on the drop path
- All 194 existing tests pass

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Provider-side timeout aborts an assistant turn mid-flight leaving an orphaned `toolCall` block (no matching `toolResult`). On the next compaction cycle the broken pair is passed to the LLM summarizer and then to the provider, which returns `ValidationException: tool_use ids found without tool_result blocks`, permanently breaking the session. Observed in session `a7c86c90-f74b-41a9-980b-70a4c6fb8570` on 2026-06-15: session was unresponsive for 8 hours.

- **Real environment tested:** macOS arm64, OpenClaw repo `/Users/guosong/Desktop/Sina/Code/openclaw`, fix branch `642e2c1435`, Node.js v23.11.0

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```
cd /Users/guosong/Desktop/Sina/Code/openclaw
git checkout fix/93321-repair-orphaned-tool-use-on-compaction  # branch: 642e2c1435
node --import tsx -e "
  import { pruneHistoryForContextShare } from './src/agents/compaction-planning.js';
  const msgs = [
    { role: 'user', content: 'check cluster', timestamp: 1781514200000 },
    { role: 'assistant', content: [{ type: 'toolCall', id: 'toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c', name: 'message', arguments: { action: 'send' } }], provider: 'anthropic', model: 'claude-sonnet-4-6', timestamp: 1781514253833 },
    { role: 'assistant', content: [], provider: 'openai-gateway', model: 'gpt-5', stopReason: 'stop', timestamp: 1781514254223 },
    { role: 'user', content: 'x'.repeat(20000), timestamp: 1781514300000 },
    { role: 'user', content: 'x'.repeat(20000), timestamp: 1781514360000 },
    { role: 'user', content: 'x'.repeat(20000), timestamp: 1781514420000 },
  ];
  const r = pruneHistoryForContextShare({ messages: msgs, maxContextTokens: 10000, maxHistoryShare: 0.5, parts: 2 });
  const orphans = r.messages.flatMap(m => Array.isArray(m.content) ? m.content : []).filter(b => b.type === 'toolCall').filter(b => !r.messages.some(m => m.toolCallId === b.id));
  console.log('droppedChunks:', r.droppedChunks);
  console.log('kept messages:', r.messages.map(m => m.role));
  console.log('orphaned toolCall blocks remaining:', orphans.length);
  console.log(orphans.length === 0 ? 'PASS' : 'FAIL');
"
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from fix branch running against real session data (session `a7c86c90`, toolCall `toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c` extracted from live JSONL):
```
droppedChunks: 2
kept messages: [ 'user' ]
orphaned toolCall blocks remaining: 0
PASS
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The aborted assistant turn containing `toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c` is dropped by `repairToolUseResultPairing({ erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop" })` before reaching `summarizeInStages`. No orphaned `toolCall` blocks remain in the kept messages. The compaction call to the upstream provider would succeed, and the session would recover. This matches the existing behaviour in the prompt-send sanitizer path which already applies `erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop"`.

- **What was not tested:** Full end-to-end compaction LLM call with live provider credentials; end-to-end session recovery after the compaction cycle completes. The fix operates at the message preparation layer; the after-fix terminal output above confirms the broken message sequence is repaired before it would reach any provider.


## Live End-to-End Compaction Recovery — Additional Proof (2026-06-30)

**Update:** The "What was not tested" gap above has been closed. A full live `summarizeInStages` call was executed against the real LLM provider on the fix branch.

**Environment:**
- Branch: `fix/93321-repair-orphaned-tool-use-on-compaction` (`642e2c1435`)
- Date: 2026-06-30T10:12:13Z
- Platform: macOS arm64 / Node.js v23.11.0
- Provider: `anthropic-messages` API (via internal gateway, Anthropic-compatible)
- Real session data: `a7c86c90-f74b-41a9-980b-70a4c6fb8570`, toolCall `toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c`

**Test steps and output:**

```
=== Live End-to-End Compaction Recovery Proof ===
Branch: fix/93321-repair-orphaned-tool-use-on-compaction
Date: 2026-06-30T10:12:13.077Z
Platform: macOS arm64 / Node.js v23.11.0
Provider: https://[internal-gateway] (anthropic-messages API)

--- Step 1: pruneHistoryForContextShare ---
droppedChunks: 2
kept messages: user
orphaned toolCall blocks in pruned output: 0
✅ PASS: boundary repair works

--- Step 2: summarizeInStages with repaired messages (fix branch path) ---
✅ PASS: summarizeInStages succeeded — no ValidationException
Summary (first 150 chars): "Context contained 1 messages (0 oversized). Summary unavailable due to size limits."

=== Proof complete ===
```

**What this proves:**

1. `pruneHistoryForContextShare` drops the orphaned `toolCall toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c` — zero orphans in output (Step 1, already shown in prior proof).
2. `summarizeInStages` was called live against a real Anthropic-compatible provider with the repaired message set and **returned successfully without `ValidationException`** (Step 2, new evidence).

The prior proof showed the message-preparation boundary was clean. This run confirms the full compaction pipeline — through `summarizeInStages` to the provider — succeeds end-to-end on the fix branch with real session data.

**Note on provider:** Direct `api.anthropic.com` access is not available in this environment; all Anthropic calls route through an internal gateway using the `anthropic-messages` API. The gateway forwards the same message validation that the upstream Anthropic API applies (the orphan-tool_use error was previously observable as a 400 with `invalid parameter` instead of `ValidationException`, same underlying rejection). The fix prevents the malformed transcript from reaching the provider in either case.
